**[MERIDIAN HEALTH SYSTEMS, INC.]{.underline}**

**[AI / Technology Vendor Negotiation Playbook --- 2025 Edition]{.underline}**

**Maintained by the Office of General Counsel** **Prepared under the direction of Dr. Priya Narayanan, SVP & General Counsel**

Classification: **PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT** Do Not Distribute Outside Meridian Legal, Procurement, and Authorized Negotiation Teams

Last Updated: June 2025

**TABLE OF CONTENTS**

> • **Section 1** --- Purpose & Scope
>
> • **Section 2** --- General Negotiation Principles
>
> • **Section 3** --- Liability & Risk Allocation
>
> • **Section 4** --- Intellectual Property Ownership
>
> • **Section 5** --- AI Transparency & Audit Rights
>
> • **Section 6** --- Data Usage & Aggregation Rights
>
> • **Section 7** --- Indemnification
>
> • **Section 8** --- Service Level Agreements & Accuracy Benchmarks
>
> • **Section 9** --- Termination & Transition
>
> • **Section 10** --- Regulatory Compliance Requirements
>
> • **Section 11** --- Insurance Requirements
>
> • **Section 12** --- Board-Approved Risk Tolerances & Escalation Procedures
>
> • **Appendix A** --- Term-by-Term Fallback Ladder Summary Matrix
>
> • **Appendix B** --- Approved Clause Bank (Selected Model Language)
>
> • **Appendix C** --- Regulatory Reference Guide

**[Section 1 --- Purpose & Scope]{.underline}**

**[1.1 --- Purpose]{.underline}**

The purpose of this Playbook is to provide Meridian Health Systems, Inc. (\"Meridian\" or the \"Company\") negotiation teams with standardized positions, fallback ladders, red lines, and board-approved risk tolerances for procuring AI-enabled and advanced technology solutions. As Meridian continues to expand its deployment of artificial intelligence across clinical decision-support, patient-facing applications, and operational workflows, the Office of General Counsel has determined that a unified, disciplined approach to vendor negotiations is essential to protect the Company\'s clinical, financial, and reputational interests.

This Playbook is not merely advisory. It establishes the authoritative framework within which all AI and advanced technology vendor negotiations must be conducted. Negotiation teams are required to adhere to the positions, fallback ladders, and escalation procedures described herein. Deviations from this Playbook are permitted only through the escalation mechanisms described in Section 12.

**[1.2 --- Intended Audience]{.underline}**

This Playbook is intended for use by the following authorized personnel:

> • **Office of General Counsel**: Dr. Priya Narayanan, SVP & General Counsel, and all attorneys within the Meridian Legal Department assigned to technology transactions.
>
> • **Digital Health & Technology Transactions**: Marcus Delgado, VP, and all procurement professionals and contract managers within the Digital Health & Technology Transactions team.
>
> • **Authorized Outside Counsel**: Whitfield Ames LLP, specifically Elaine Sutter, Partner, and members of her team assigned to Meridian engagements.
>
> • **Designated Procurement Leads**: Any procurement professional authorized by Marcus Delgado to participate in AI/technology vendor negotiations on behalf of Meridian.

No other Meridian personnel are authorized to access or rely upon this Playbook without the express written consent of Dr. Priya Narayanan.

**[1.3 --- Scope of Application]{.underline}**

This Playbook applies to all AI/technology vendor agreements where any one or more of the following criteria are met:

> \(a\) The agreement involves the deployment of **clinical decision-support or patient-facing AI** systems, including but not limited to sepsis prediction models, radiology triage algorithms, medication-interaction alert systems, natural language processing tools for clinical documentation, and any AI system that generates outputs used by clinicians in the course of patient care.
>
> \(b\) The **annual contract value (ACV)** of the agreement exceeds **\$1,000,000**, regardless of whether the solution involves AI or advanced technology as defined above.
>
> \(c\) The solution **processes protected health information (PHI)** as defined under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (\"HIPAA\") and the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act (\"HITECH Act\") across any of Meridian\'s 14 hospitals located in Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey.

Where an agreement meets more than one of the criteria described above, the most protective provisions of this Playbook shall apply. In circumstances where the Playbook\'s scope is ambiguous or where a novel technology deployment does not fit neatly within the categories described above, negotiation teams should consult the Office of General Counsel for guidance before proceeding.

**[1.4 --- Meridian\'s Risk Profile]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s annual patient encounter volume is approximately **2.1 million** across its 14-hospital network spanning three states. The Company\'s Named Licensed User population for enterprise technology platforms stands at approximately **6,200** clinicians, administrators, and support personnel. These figures are significant because they amplify Meridian\'s risk exposure in any AI/technology deployment. A clinical decision-support tool that produces erroneous outputs at scale across a 14-hospital network serving 2.1 million patient encounters annually presents fundamentally different risk characteristics than the same tool deployed at a single-site facility. Meridian\'s insistence on protective terms throughout this Playbook is grounded in this risk reality and should be communicated to vendors as such.

**[1.5 --- Key Regulatory and Governance Developments]{.underline}**

This 2025 Edition of the Playbook incorporates several significant developments since the prior edition:

> \(a\) **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act** (effective March 1, 2025): This legislation mandates independent annual audits of AI systems used in clinical decision-making at healthcare facilities in New Jersey. Meridian operates three hospitals subject to this Act. Non-compliance exposes the Company to civil penalties of up to \$50,000 per violation. The Act\'s requirements are reflected throughout this Playbook, particularly in Section 5 (AI Transparency & Audit Rights) and Section 10 (Regulatory Compliance Requirements).
>
> \(b\) **Board Risk Committee --- Tier 1 Enterprise Risk Classification**: In January 2025, the Meridian Board Risk Committee formally designated AI-related liability as a Tier 1 enterprise risk. This designation carries specific escalation and approval requirements described in Section 12 and reflects the Board\'s judgment that AI deployment in clinical settings presents risks comparable to the Company\'s highest-severity risk categories.
>
> \(c\) **Evolving FDA Guidance**: The Food and Drug Administration continues to refine its regulatory framework for clinical decision-support software. While the full scope of future FDA regulation remains uncertain, this Playbook incorporates provisions designed to accommodate evolving FDA requirements, including vendor obligations to maintain necessary clearances and to cooperate with regulatory inquiries.

**[1.6 --- Privilege Designation]{.underline}**

This Playbook is protected by the **attorney-client privilege** and the **work-product doctrine**. It was prepared at the direction of Dr. Priya Narayanan, SVP & General Counsel, for the purpose of providing legal advice to Meridian in connection with anticipated and ongoing vendor negotiations. The Playbook reflects the mental impressions, conclusions, opinions, and legal theories of Meridian\'s in-house and outside counsel.

This Playbook must not be disclosed to any vendor, counterparty, or external party other than Whitfield Ames LLP. Inadvertent disclosure does not constitute waiver of privilege, and any recipient of an inadvertent disclosure is instructed to return or destroy all copies immediately and to notify the Office of General Counsel. All copies of this Playbook, whether in electronic or physical form, must be maintained in accordance with Meridian\'s Information Security and Document Retention Policies.

**[Section 2 --- General Negotiation Principles]{.underline}**

This Section establishes the foundational principles that govern all AI/technology vendor negotiations conducted under this Playbook. These principles apply across all term categories and should inform the negotiation team\'s approach at every stage of the engagement.

**[2.1 --- Governing Law]{.underline}**

**Standard Position.** Meridian\'s standard position is that all AI/technology agreements shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the **State of Delaware**, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles. Delaware governing law provides Meridian with a well-developed body of commercial contract law, predictable judicial interpretation, and strong protections for technology licensees. Delaware is also the state of Meridian\'s incorporation, and consistency of governing law across the Company\'s vendor agreements reduces legal complexity and cost.

**Fallback.** In limited circumstances, the vendor may insist on its home-state law as the governing law of the agreement. Meridian\'s negotiation team may accept an alternative governing law only where **all** of the following conditions are met:

> \(a\) Delaware governing law is genuinely unavailable---for example, because the vendor has a legitimate policy of contracting exclusively under its home-state law and has demonstrated that this policy is applied uniformly to all customers, not selectively to Meridian.
>
> \(b\) The alternative jurisdiction does not **materially weaken** Meridian\'s position on liability caps, indemnification, or data-protection remedies. Before accepting an alternative governing law, the negotiation team must consult with the Office of General Counsel or Whitfield Ames LLP to confirm that the alternative jurisdiction\'s substantive law does not undermine the protections established elsewhere in this Playbook.
>
> \(c\) The negotiation team has obtained written confirmation from the Office of General Counsel that the alternative governing law is acceptable for the specific engagement.

Under no circumstances should a negotiation team accept governing law from a jurisdiction that imposes statutory limitations on consequential damages, restricts indemnification obligations, or otherwise limits the enforceability of the protective provisions described in this Playbook, without GC approval.

**[2.2 --- Dispute Resolution]{.underline}**

**Standard Position.** All disputes arising under or in connection with the agreement shall be resolved by **binding arbitration** administered by the American Arbitration Association (\"AAA\") under its Commercial Arbitration Rules, seated in **Wilmington, Delaware**. The arbitration shall be conducted by a panel of three arbitrators, each of whom shall have experience with healthcare technology transactions. The arbitral award shall be final and binding and may be entered as a judgment in any court of competent jurisdiction.

**Carve-Out for Injunctive Relief.** Notwithstanding the arbitration provision, either party shall have the right to seek temporary, preliminary, or permanent injunctive relief in **any court of competent jurisdiction** with respect to the following categories of claims, without the necessity of posting a bond or other security:

> \(a\) Claims involving the misappropriation or unauthorized use of intellectual property;
>
> \(b\) Claims involving an actual or threatened breach of data security obligations or unauthorized disclosure of protected health information;
>
> \(c\) Claims involving an actual or imminent threat to patient safety arising from the AI platform\'s outputs or operations.

The injunctive-relief carve-out is essential because arbitration timelines are typically insufficient to address the urgent nature of IP theft, data breaches, or patient-safety emergencies. Negotiation teams should resist any vendor attempt to eliminate or narrow this carve-out.

**[2.3 --- Most-Favored-Customer Pricing]{.underline}**

**Standard Position.** Every AI/technology agreement must include a **most-favored-customer (\"MFC\") clause** ensuring that Meridian receives pricing no less favorable than that offered to any similarly situated health-system customer of the vendor. For purposes of the MFC clause, \"similarly situated\" means a health system with comparable annual patient encounter volume (1.5 million or more), a comparable number of Named Licensed Users, and a comparable scope of deployment.

The MFC clause must cover the following fee categories:

> \(a\) **Base subscription or license fees**, including per-user, per-encounter, and platform fees;
>
> \(b\) **Implementation fees**, including configuration, integration, training, and go-live support;
>
> \(c\) **Renewal terms**, including pricing escalators, renewal-term length, and any discounts or credits offered in connection with renewal.

If Meridian discovers that the vendor has offered more favorable pricing to a similarly situated customer, the vendor must retroactively adjust Meridian\'s fees to match the more favorable pricing from the date the more favorable pricing was first offered to the other customer. The MFC clause must include a reasonable audit right enabling Meridian to verify the vendor\'s compliance.

Negotiation teams should be aware that vendors frequently attempt to limit the MFC clause by defining \"similarly situated\" narrowly (e.g., requiring identical deployment configurations) or by excluding certain fee categories. These limitations should be resisted.

**[2.4 --- Data Portability]{.underline}**

**Standard Position.** Every agreement must guarantee the export of all Customer Data---defined to include all data created, collected, processed, or stored by or on behalf of Meridian in connection with the agreement---in industry-standard interoperable formats. Specifically, the agreement must require the vendor to support data export in both **FHIR R4** (Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources, Release 4) and **HL7 v2** (Health Level Seven International, Version 2) formats.

Data portability is not merely a termination-related concern. Meridian must have the ability to export its data at any time during the term of the agreement for purposes of interoperability, regulatory compliance, quality assurance, and integration with other systems in Meridian\'s technology ecosystem. Accordingly, the data portability obligation must apply both during the term and following termination or expiration of the agreement for any reason.

The vendor must provide data export tools or APIs that enable Meridian to perform bulk data exports without vendor assistance, or, if vendor assistance is required, the vendor must provide such assistance within a commercially reasonable timeframe at no additional charge. Data portability obligations must **survive termination or expiration** of the agreement.

**[2.5 --- Negotiation Priority Ranking]{.underline}**

The Office of General Counsel and the Board Risk Committee have approved the following priority ranking for AI/technology procurements. This ranking governs the order in which negotiation teams should allocate negotiating capital and make trade-off decisions:

> 1\. **Patient-safety liability protections** --- The single most important term category. No concession on patient-safety liability may be made to gain advantage on any other term.
>
> 2\. **Audit rights (AI transparency)** --- Driven by regulatory requirements (NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act) and Meridian\'s need to understand and validate AI system behavior in clinical settings.
>
> 3\. **Termination flexibility and transition support** --- Meridian must never be locked into a non-performing or unsuitable vendor relationship due to prohibitive exit costs.
>
> 4\. **Data usage / aggregation restrictions** --- Meridian\'s data is exceptionally valuable and must be protected from unrestricted vendor exploitation.
>
> 5\. **SLA accuracy benchmarks** --- Binding performance commitments are essential for clinical decision-support tools, but are ranked below the structural protections in priorities 1--4.
>
> 6\. **IP ownership** --- Important for long-term strategic flexibility, but secondary to liability and operational protections.
>
> 7\. **Indemnification scope** --- While indemnification is a meaningful protection, it is the most readily tradeable of the seven priority categories.

**Critical Rule.** Negotiators must not concede on a higher-priority item to gain advantage on a lower-priority item without the express approval of the General Counsel. For example, a negotiation team may not accept a reduced patient-safety liability cap (Priority 1) in exchange for more favorable IP ownership terms (Priority 6). This rule applies regardless of the perceived commercial value of the trade-off.

In practice, the priority ranking is most relevant during the final stages of negotiation, when vendors frequently propose package deals or trade-offs. Negotiation teams should be prepared to explain Meridian\'s priority framework to vendors in general terms (without disclosing this Playbook or its specific fallback ladders) as a means of focusing the negotiation on areas of genuine flexibility.

**[2.6 --- Board Briefing Requirement]{.underline}**

As noted in Section 1, the Board Risk Committee has classified AI-related liability as a **Tier 1 enterprise risk**. In connection with this classification, the Board has established the following requirement:

Any AI/technology agreement with a **total contract value (TCV) exceeding \$10 million** must be briefed to the Board Risk Committee before execution. The briefing must include, at a minimum:

> \(a\) A summary of the key commercial terms, including ACV, TCV, and term length;
>
> \(b\) A summary of the liability and risk allocation terms, including the general liability cap, super-cap structure, and any deviations from the standard position described in this Playbook;
>
> \(c\) A description of the AI platform\'s clinical applications and the patient populations affected;
>
> \(d\) An assessment of regulatory compliance, including compliance with the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act, HIPAA, and any applicable FDA requirements;
>
> \(e\) A recommendation from the Office of General Counsel regarding execution.

The Board Risk Committee meets monthly and may convene special sessions for time-sensitive approvals. Negotiation teams should build the Board briefing timeline into the overall deal timeline to avoid delays at execution.

**[Section 3 --- Liability & Risk Allocation]{.underline}**

Liability and risk allocation is the single most consequential section of any AI/technology vendor agreement. The terms described in this Section directly affect Meridian\'s financial exposure in the event of a system failure, data breach, or patient-safety incident. Given the Board Risk Committee\'s Tier 1 enterprise risk designation, the Office of General Counsel has established detailed fallback ladders and absolute floors for each component of the liability framework. Negotiation teams must adhere strictly to this Section.

**[3.1 --- General Liability Cap]{.underline}**

The general liability cap sets the maximum aggregate liability of each party under the agreement, subject to the carve-outs and super-caps described in Section 3.2. The general cap typically applies to claims such as breach of contract, breach of warranty, negligence (other than gross negligence), and other general commercial claims.

**3.1.1 --- Standard Position**

Meridian\'s standard position is a general liability cap set at **two times (2×) the trailing twelve-month fees** paid or payable under the agreement (i.e., 2× ACV). For an engagement with an ACV of \$9.6 million---representative of Meridian\'s current clinical AI platform procurements---this equates to a general liability cap of approximately **\$19.2 million**.

The trailing-twelve-month measurement methodology is preferred because it captures the actual economic relationship between the parties at the time a claim arises, rather than a fixed amount that may become stale over a multi-year engagement. If the vendor proposes an alternative measurement methodology (e.g., total fees paid to date, or a fixed dollar amount), negotiate toward the trailing-twelve-month approach but evaluate the alternative on its merits in consultation with the Office of General Counsel.

**3.1.2 --- First Fallback**

If the vendor resists the 2× ACV standard position, the negotiation team is authorized to negotiate down to **one and three-quarters times (1.75×) the trailing twelve-month fees**, which equates to approximately **\$16.8 million** at a \$9.6 million ACV. This fallback may be offered only if it is accompanied by an acceptable super-cap structure for carve-out claims, as described in Section 3.2.2. The purpose of this linkage is to ensure that any reduction in the general cap is offset by meaningful protection on the highest-severity claims (patient safety, PHI breach, willful misconduct, IP infringement).

**3.1.3 --- Second Fallback**

If the vendor continues to resist, the negotiation team may accept **one and one-half times (1.5×) the trailing twelve-month fees**, which equates to approximately **\$14.4 million** at a \$9.6 million ACV. This is the lowest general cap Meridian will accept. This fallback is available only if the vendor agrees to a super-cap of **four times (4×) ACV** for all carve-out categories, as described in Section 3.2.3. The rationale is straightforward: the lower the general cap, the more critical it is that the most severe categories of liability are protected by a robust super-cap.

**3.1.4 --- Absolute Floor**

The general liability cap must **never fall below 1.5× ACV** under any circumstances. Any vendor proposal that would result in a general cap below this threshold---including, for example, a general cap of 1× ACV, a fixed-dollar cap below 1.5× ACV, or a cap based on fees paid to date rather than trailing twelve-month fees---must be **rejected outright** or escalated to Dr. Priya Narayanan for GC-level override authority.

Negotiators do not have discretion to accept a general cap below 1.5× ACV under any circumstances. This floor was established by the Board Risk Committee in connection with its Tier 1 enterprise risk designation and reflects the Board\'s judgment that a general cap below 1.5× ACV would leave Meridian with insufficient financial protection in the event of a significant system failure or commercial dispute.

**[3.2 --- Super-Cap / Carve-Out Liability]{.underline}**

Certain categories of liability are excluded from the general liability cap because they represent the most severe and potentially catastrophic risks arising from an AI/technology vendor relationship. The treatment of these carve-out categories is described below.

**3.2.1 --- Standard Position (Uncapped Carve-Outs)**

Meridian\'s standard position is that the following categories of liability are **uncapped**---that is, they are excluded entirely from the general liability cap and are not subject to any separate dollar limitation:

> \(a\) **HIPAA / PHI breaches**: Any liability arising from the unauthorized access, use, disclosure, or loss of protected health information, including but not limited to breach notification costs, regulatory fines and penalties, credit monitoring, and third-party claims.
>
> \(b\) **Willful misconduct or gross negligence**: Any liability arising from the willful misconduct or gross negligence of the vendor, its employees, agents, or subcontractors.
>
> \(c\) **Patient-safety claims**: Any liability arising from bodily injury, clinical misdiagnosis, treatment errors, or other patient harm attributable to AI-generated outputs, recommendations, or alerts produced by the vendor\'s platform.
>
> \(d\) **IP infringement indemnification obligations**: Any liability arising from the vendor\'s obligation to indemnify Meridian for claims of intellectual property infringement, as described in Section 7.

**Rationale.** Meridian\'s 14-hospital network serves approximately 2.1 million patient encounters annually. At this scale, the potential magnitude of patient-safety and PHI-breach claims is substantial and inherently difficult to predict. A cap on these categories of liability would force Meridian to absorb losses above the cap amount, effectively transferring catastrophic risk from the party best positioned to prevent it (the vendor, as the designer and operator of the AI platform) to the party least able to control it (Meridian, as the customer). This risk allocation is inappropriate and must be resisted.

**3.2.2 --- First Fallback (Super-Cap at 3× ACV for Patient-Safety)**

If the vendor insists that some ceiling must apply to carve-out claims---a position that is common among venture-backed AI companies and their investors---the negotiation team may accept a **super-cap of three times (3×) ACV** (approximately **\$28.8 million** at a \$9.6 million ACV), but only for **patient-safety claims** as defined in Section 3.2.1(c).

Under this fallback, the following categories remain uncapped:

> • HIPAA / PHI breaches
>
> • Willful misconduct or gross negligence
>
> • IP infringement indemnification obligations

This fallback may be offered only if the general liability cap is held at **1.75× ACV or higher** (i.e., the first fallback or standard position on the general cap). The pairing of a 3× ACV patient-safety super-cap with a general cap below 1.75× ACV would leave Meridian with an aggregate liability framework that the Office of General Counsel considers inadequate.

**3.2.3 --- Second Fallback (Super-Cap at 4× ACV for All Carve-Outs)**

If the vendor is unwilling to accept any uncapped categories, the negotiation team may accept a **super-cap of four times (4×) ACV** (approximately **\$38.4 million** at a \$9.6 million ACV) applied across **all carve-out categories**: HIPAA/PHI breaches, willful misconduct, patient-safety claims, and IP infringement indemnification obligations.

This is a significant concession, and it is available only when paired with a general cap at **1.5× ACV** (the absolute floor on the general cap). The purpose of the 4× ACV super-cap is to provide meaningful headroom above the general cap for the most severe claims, even where the vendor has successfully negotiated the general cap to its lowest permissible level.

**3.2.4 --- Absolute Floor --- Patient-Safety Liability**

**Patient-safety liability must never be capped below 3× ACV** (approximately \$28.8 million at a \$9.6 million ACV). This is a **non-negotiable red line** established by the Board Risk Committee in connection with its Tier 1 enterprise risk classification. The Board\'s reasoning is that patient-safety claims arising from AI-generated clinical outputs represent the single greatest reputational, financial, and operational risk associated with AI deployment in a clinical setting, and that any cap below 3× ACV would be inconsistent with the Company\'s obligations to its patients and its enterprise risk management framework.

If a vendor refuses to accept a patient-safety floor of 3× ACV, the deal must be escalated to Dr. Priya Narayanan. If Dr. Narayanan is unable to resolve the impasse through direct negotiation with the vendor\'s leadership, the matter must be escalated to the Board Risk Committee before the Company may proceed. Negotiation teams do not have authority to waive or reduce this floor under any circumstances.

**[Section 4 --- Intellectual Property Ownership]{.underline}**

**[4.1 --- Guiding Principle]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s clinical data, operational workflows, clinician feedback, and institutional knowledge contribute substantial value to any AI model that is fine-tuned or adapted using Meridian-specific inputs. Vendor base models are general-purpose tools; it is Meridian\'s data and clinical context that transform those models into high-value, specialized applications. Ownership terms must reflect this contribution and, critically, must ensure that Meridian is never locked into a single vendor due to the inability to access or port model adaptations.

The strategic risk of vendor lock-in is particularly acute in the AI context because fine-tuned model weights and derivative data may be difficult or impossible to recreate without access to the original training environment and data. If a vendor retains exclusive ownership of these assets and Meridian lacks portability rights, the cost of switching vendors may become prohibitive---effectively converting a contractual relationship into a dependency. The IP provisions described in this Section are designed to prevent that outcome.

**[4.2 --- Standard Position --- Customer Ownership]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s standard position is as follows:

> \(a\) **Meridian owns all fine-tuned model weights and derivative data** generated through the use of Meridian\'s data, clinical workflows, clinician feedback, or other Meridian-specific inputs. \"Fine-tuned model weights\" means the parameters of any AI model that have been adjusted, retrained, or otherwise modified using Meridian Data. \"Derivative data\" means any data created as a result of the processing of Meridian Data through the vendor\'s platform, including model outputs, performance metrics, and validation results.
>
> \(b\) **Vendor retains ownership of its pre-existing base model**, platform architecture, proprietary algorithms, and all intellectual property that existed prior to or independent of the engagement with Meridian. Meridian does not claim ownership of the vendor\'s foundational technology.
>
> \(c\) **Vendor receives a non-exclusive, royalty-free license** to use de-identified, aggregated learnings from the fine-tuning process solely for the purpose of improving the vendor\'s base model and platform. This license is subject to Meridian\'s data-usage restrictions as described in Section 6 of this Playbook, and does not authorize the vendor to use any Meridian Data in identified or identifiable form or to provide Meridian-specific learnings to any third party.

This allocation reflects the principle that each party should own what it contributed: the vendor owns its pre-existing technology, and Meridian owns the value created through the application of its data and clinical context.

**[4.3 --- First Fallback --- Joint Ownership with Customer Exclusive-Use License]{.underline}**

If the vendor refuses to accept Customer ownership of fine-tuned models---a resistance point that is common among AI vendors that view fine-tuned model weights as extensions of their proprietary technology---the negotiation team may propose **joint ownership** with the following structure:

> \(a\) Meridian and Vendor jointly own all fine-tuned model weights and derivative data.
>
> \(b\) Meridian receives an **exclusive-use license** for all healthcare applications within Meridian\'s service area, defined as the states of **Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey**. Within this territory, no other healthcare system or provider may benefit from Meridian\'s fine-tuned model weights or derivative data.
>
> \(c\) Vendor may use the jointly-owned fine-tuned materials only **outside Meridian\'s exclusive territory** and only in **de-identified, aggregated form**. The vendor may not provide any Meridian-specific model adaptations to a competing health system within Meridian\'s three-state service area.
>
> \(d\) Both parties have the right to independently exploit the jointly-owned materials within the scope of their respective licenses, without accounting to the other party.

This fallback preserves Meridian\'s competitive advantage within its service area while giving the vendor the ability to leverage general learnings from the engagement for model improvement in other markets.

**[4.4 --- Second Fallback --- Vendor Ownership with Irrevocable Perpetual License]{.underline}**

If joint ownership is unacceptable to the vendor, the negotiation team may accept **Vendor ownership** of fine-tuned model weights and adaptations, provided Meridian receives the following rights:

> \(a\) An **irrevocable, perpetual, fully paid-up license** to use, copy, modify, and create derivative works from all fine-tuned model weights and derivative data generated through the use of Meridian\'s data, clinical workflows, or feedback. This license must be irrevocable, meaning it cannot be terminated by the vendor for any reason, including breach of the underlying agreement.
>
> \(b\) Full **portability rights**, including the right to export fine-tuned model weights and run them on alternative infrastructure, with a successor vendor, or on Meridian-owned computing resources. The vendor must provide all technical documentation and assistance reasonably necessary to enable Meridian to exercise its portability rights.
>
> \(c\) The license and portability rights described above must **survive termination or expiration** of the agreement for any reason, including termination for cause by the vendor. The survival of these rights is non-negotiable.

This is the **final fallback** approved by the Office of General Counsel for IP ownership terms. No position below this level may be accepted by the negotiation team. If the vendor refuses to grant an irrevocable perpetual license with full portability rights, the matter must be escalated to Dr. Priya Narayanan for GC-level evaluation and, if necessary, deal termination.

**[4.5 --- Red Line --- Model Weights Access]{.underline}**

Vendors frequently assert that providing access to raw model weights is a \"hard no,\" citing concerns about intellectual property protection, competitive advantage, and the risk of reverse engineering. Negotiation teams should be prepared for this resistance and should respond as follows:

> \(a\) Meridian\'s perpetual license and portability rights under Section 4.4 **do not require Meridian to possess raw base-model weights**. Meridian acknowledges that the vendor\'s pre-existing base model is the vendor\'s proprietary technology and does not seek ownership of or access to base-model weights.
>
> \(b\) However, the perpetual license and portability rights **must encompass all fine-tuned weights and adaptations**---that is, the delta between the base model and the fine-tuned model that was created using Meridian\'s data and clinical inputs.
>
> \(c\) If a vendor **conflates base-model weights with fine-tuned weights** in order to deny Meridian access to the fine-tuned layer, negotiators must **clarify and hold the distinction**. The vendor\'s base model and Meridian\'s fine-tuned adaptations are technically and legally distinct assets. Negotiators should insist on contract language that clearly delineates the two categories and applies the appropriate ownership and licensing terms to each.

This distinction is critical and often becomes a point of confusion or deliberate obfuscation during negotiations. Negotiation teams should engage Meridian\'s technical advisors or Whitfield Ames LLP if the vendor\'s technical team disputes the feasibility of separating fine-tuned weights from base-model weights.

**[Section 5 --- AI Transparency & Audit Rights]{.underline}**

**[5.1 --- Regulatory Context]{.underline}**

The right to audit and inspect AI systems deployed in clinical settings is both a regulatory imperative and a core risk management function. Two regulatory developments are particularly relevant to this Section.

**NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (effective March 1, 2025).** This Act mandates that all healthcare facilities in New Jersey deploying AI systems in clinical decision-making must subject those systems to **independent annual audits**. Audit reports must be available to the New Jersey Department of Health upon request. Non-compliance exposes Meridian to **civil penalties of up to \$50,000 per violation**. Meridian operates **three hospitals in New Jersey** that are subject to this Act: Meridian Atlantic Medical Center (Atlantic City), Meridian South Jersey Regional Hospital (Egg Harbor Township), and Meridian Princeton Medical Center (Plainsboro). The audit provisions in every AI/technology agreement must, at a minimum, satisfy the requirements of the NJ Act.

**FDA Oversight of Clinical Decision-Support Software.** The Food and Drug Administration has increasing regulatory oversight of clinical decision-support software, and may impose additional transparency and audit requirements as its regulatory framework evolves. All audit provisions must be drafted with sufficient flexibility to accommodate evolving FDA guidance without requiring contract amendment. Specifically, audit clauses should grant Meridian the right to conduct audits \"as required by applicable law or regulation, including but not limited to the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act and any FDA regulation applicable to clinical decision-support software.\"

**[5.2 --- Standard Position --- Quarterly Audits with Model-Internals Access]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s standard position requires the following audit rights:

> \(a\) Meridian, or its authorized agents (including outside counsel, internal audit staff, and pre-approved third-party auditors), may conduct **quarterly audits** of the vendor\'s AI platform.
>
> \(b\) The scope of each audit includes access to **model internals**, defined as: architecture documentation (including neural network topology, layer descriptions, and activation functions); training data provenance (including source, vintage, demographic composition, and curation methodology); validation results (including sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value across defined patient subpopulations); test query access (the ability to submit defined test cases to the model and observe outputs); and summary performance metrics (including model drift metrics, feature importance rankings, and error analysis).
>
> \(c\) The vendor must provide access to the information and systems described above within **10 business days** of Meridian\'s written audit request.
>
> \(d\) Audits may be conducted remotely or on-site at Meridian\'s election, and the vendor shall cooperate fully with all reasonable audit procedures.

Quarterly audits at this level of access provide Meridian with the most robust transparency framework and enable the Company to detect and respond to model drift, bias, and accuracy degradation on a timely basis. This is the preferred position and should be pursued vigorously in all negotiations.

**[5.3 --- First Fallback --- Semi-Annual Audits with Summary and Detail Reports]{.underline}**

If the vendor resists the quarterly audit cadence---citing, for example, operational burden, disruption to engineering resources, or proprietary-information concerns---the negotiation team may accept a reduced frequency of **semi-annual** audits, subject to the following conditions:

> \(a\) Each audit must produce two deliverables: (i) a **summary report** suitable for presentation to Meridian\'s Board Risk Committee and for regulatory filings (including filings under the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act), and (ii) a **detailed technical report** covering model architecture, training data provenance, validation results, and test queries.
>
> \(b\) The access level corresponds to **Langford Tier 2 access**: model architecture documentation, training data provenance, validation results, and test queries, without requiring access to raw model weights or embeddings.
>
> \(c\) The vendor must provide access within **10 business days** of Meridian\'s written audit request, consistent with the standard position.
>
> \(d\) Meridian retains the right to convert to quarterly audits upon 30 days\' written notice if a material performance deficiency, regulatory inquiry, or patient-safety concern is identified.

This fallback reduces the vendor\'s audit burden while preserving Meridian\'s access to the information necessary for regulatory compliance and risk management. The conversion right in clause (d) above provides an important safety valve.

**[5.4 --- Second Fallback --- Annual Audits with Independent Auditor Under NDA]{.underline}**

This is the **minimum acceptable position** on audit rights. It may be offered only when higher-priority terms (patient-safety liability, termination flexibility) are at stake and the negotiation team has exhausted other options.

Under this fallback:

> \(a\) Meridian is entitled to an **annual audit** of the vendor\'s AI platform, conducted by a pre-approved independent auditor operating under a separate NDA with the vendor and subject to secure-environment-only access protocols.
>
> \(b\) The **pre-approved auditor** is **Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC** (Dr. Anil Cherian, Managing Director), 101 Federal Street, Suite 1900, Boston, MA 02110. Langford has been vetted by the Office of General Counsel and Whitfield Ames LLP and has been determined to possess the technical capabilities, independence, and confidentiality safeguards necessary for this engagement. No other auditor may be substituted without GC approval.
>
> \(c\) **Langford confidentiality protocols** include: execution of a separate NDA with the vendor prior to any audit; access limited to a secure environment designated by the vendor (which may include a virtual data room or on-premises clean room); no data extraction from the secure environment; all work product destroyed after delivery of the final audit report to Meridian, with destruction certified in writing by Langford.
>
> \(d\) The annual audit cadence satisfies the **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act\'s minimum requirement** of an annual independent audit. However, negotiation teams should confirm with the Office of General Counsel that the specific audit scope and report format satisfy the Act\'s requirements for each agreement.
>
> \(e\) **For-cause investigations**: Notwithstanding the annual cadence, Meridian must retain the right to request an additional audit at any time in connection with a for-cause investigation, regulatory inquiry, or patient-safety concern. For-cause audits shall be conducted by Langford under **Tier 3 access** protocols: access to model weights and embeddings in a secure enclave, no data extraction, with all findings reported exclusively to Meridian\'s Office of General Counsel.

**[5.5 --- Vendor Resistance --- Tactical Guidance]{.underline}**

Negotiation teams should anticipate the following common vendor resistance points and be prepared to respond:

**\"Model-weight access is a hard no.\"** This is the most common objection. Negotiators should note that **Langford Tier 2 access** (architecture, provenance, validation, test queries) is sufficient for most regulatory compliance purposes and **does not require raw model weights**. Use this as a bridge when vendors signal flexibility on a qualified third-party audit but resist direct Customer access to internals. By framing the audit as Tier 2 access through an independent auditor under NDA, the negotiation team can address the vendor\'s proprietary-information concerns while preserving Meridian\'s transparency requirements.

**\"We already publish a model card / transparency report.\"** Vendor-published transparency reports are not a substitute for independent audit rights. These reports are self-authored, unverified, and typically lack the depth of technical detail necessary for regulatory compliance or clinical risk assessment. Negotiators should acknowledge the vendor\'s transparency efforts while making clear that independent audit rights are a separate and non-negotiable requirement.

**\"No customer has ever asked for this.\"** This objection is frequently inaccurate and, in any event, irrelevant to Meridian\'s regulatory obligations under the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act. Negotiators may reference the Act\'s requirements (without disclosing this Playbook) as the primary driver of the audit provision.

**Tactical note on preserving negotiating capital.** If the vendor has already signaled willingness to accept a qualified third-party auditor, proceed directly to the annual independent-auditor fallback (Section 5.4) to preserve negotiating capital for higher-priority terms (patient-safety liability, termination flexibility). There is no strategic value in spending negotiating capital to achieve quarterly audits if semi-annual or annual audits with independent-auditor access are sufficient for regulatory compliance and risk management, and if concessions on audit frequency can be traded for meaningful gains on Priority 1 or Priority 3 terms.

**[Section 6 --- Data Usage & Aggregation Rights]{.underline}**

**[6.1 --- Strategic Importance]{.underline}**

Data-usage and aggregation rights represent one of the most commercially significant and frequently contested areas of AI vendor negotiations. Vendors serving health systems routinely seek broad or unrestricted rights to use aggregated and de-identified patient data for model training, benchmarking, product development, and commercial purposes. For many AI vendors---particularly those in early-growth stages---access to customer data is the primary source of long-term enterprise value, often exceeding the value of the subscription revenue itself.

For a 14-hospital network generating approximately **2.1 million patient encounters annually**, Meridian\'s data is exceptionally valuable. The breadth, diversity, and clinical richness of Meridian\'s data set is a significant contributor to any fine-tuning or model-improvement activity performed by the vendor. Meridian must ensure that this value is not appropriated without adequate consideration and control.

Data-usage terms are the **fourth-highest negotiation priority** as established in Section 2.5. At the same time, data-usage terms are often the **vendor\'s single highest-priority commercial term**, because the ability to use customer data for model improvement directly affects the vendor\'s product roadmap and competitive positioning. This asymmetry creates negotiating opportunities: Meridian may be able to trade flexibility on data-usage terms for meaningful vendor concessions on higher-priority items (patient-safety liability, audit rights, termination flexibility), subject to the red lines described below.

**[6.2 --- Standard Position --- No Use Without Affirmative Written Consent]{.underline}**

Meridian\'s standard position is as follows:

> \(a\) The vendor may not use, aggregate, de-identify, or otherwise process Customer Data for **any purpose other than performing the contracted services** without **affirmative written consent** from Meridian, granted on a per-use-case basis.
>
> \(b\) \"Customer Data\" is defined broadly to include all data created, collected, generated, received, processed, or stored by or on behalf of Meridian in connection with the agreement, including but not limited to PHI, operational data, clinical workflow data, clinician feedback, utilization data, and any outputs or derivative data generated by the vendor\'s platform using Meridian inputs.
>
> \(c\) There is **no implied license** and no blanket authorization for the vendor to use Customer Data for any purpose beyond the performance of the contracted services. Any vendor form contract language purporting to grant the vendor broad rights to use, aggregate, or de-identify Customer Data must be struck during redlining.
>
> \(d\) Per-use-case consent means that each proposed use of Customer Data beyond the contracted services must be described in writing by the vendor, submitted to Meridian\'s Office of General Counsel for review, and approved in writing by an authorized signatory of Meridian before the vendor may proceed.

This position gives Meridian maximum control over its data and ensures that any value derived from Meridian\'s data for purposes beyond the contracted services is subject to Meridian\'s informed consent.

**[6.3 --- Fallback --- Opt-Out with 30-Day Notice and Verified Deletion]{.underline}**

If the vendor insists on some aggregation right as a condition of the engagement---a position that is common and, in some cases, tied to the vendor\'s pricing model---Meridian may accept the following fallback:

> \(a\) The vendor may use **aggregated, de-identified Customer Data** for model improvement and benchmarking purposes only, subject to Meridian\'s right to **opt out on 30 days\' written notice** at any time during the term of the agreement.
>
> \(b\) Upon Meridian\'s exercise of the opt-out right, the vendor must perform **verified deletion** of all Meridian-derived data, including data already incorporated into training sets, model weights, or other derivative works, within **60 days** of receipt of the opt-out notice. The vendor must certify deletion in writing, signed by an officer of the vendor, within 5 business days of completion.
>
> \(c\) The opt-out right is not subject to any cure period, notice of deficiency, or vendor consent. Meridian may exercise the opt-out right for any reason or no reason.
>
> \(d\) \"Aggregated, de-identified\" means that the data has been processed to remove all 18 HIPAA identifiers and cannot reasonably be used to identify any individual patient, and that the data is combined with data from at least two other unaffiliated customers before use by the vendor.

**Key Distinction from Vendor Standard Terms.** Vendor standard contracts typically propose 90-day opt-out windows and **prospective-only deletion** (i.e., the vendor deletes Meridian data going forward but does not delete data already incorporated into training sets or model weights). Meridian\'s fallback requires **verified deletion including data already incorporated**. This is a critical distinction.

If the vendor counters with a 60-day opt-out period or proposes prospective-only deletion, the matter must be escalated to Marcus Delgado, VP, Digital Health & Technology Transactions, or directly to the General Counsel. Negotiation teams do not have authority to accept opt-out periods exceeding 30 days or to accept purely prospective deletion obligations.

**[6.4 --- Red Lines]{.underline}**

The following are non-negotiable red lines on data-usage terms:

> \(a\) Under **no circumstances** accept unrestricted use of aggregated or de-identified Customer Data. \"Unrestricted\" means any vendor right that does not include a meaningful opt-out mechanism or that permits the vendor to use Customer Data for purposes beyond model improvement and benchmarking (e.g., sale to third parties, use in unrelated product lines, or marketing purposes).
>
> \(b\) Under **no circumstances** accept a purely prospective deletion obligation with no mechanism to remove data already incorporated into vendor models, training sets, or derivative works. If deletion of incorporated data is technically infeasible, the vendor must demonstrate this infeasibility to Meridian\'s satisfaction and propose an alternative mechanism (e.g., retraining the model without Meridian data within a specified timeframe) that achieves equivalent protection.
>
> \(c\) The **opt-out notice period must not exceed 30 days**. Longer opt-out periods undermine the practical effectiveness of the opt-out right by allowing the vendor to continue using Meridian data for an extended period after Meridian has decided to withdraw consent.

  ----------------- ------------------------------------------------ ------------------------------ ----------------------------------------------------------------
  Standard          None without affirmative written consent         Per-use-case consent           N/A

  Fallback          Aggregated/de-identified for model improvement   30-day opt-out notice          Verified deletion (including incorporated data) within 60 days

  Red Line          No unrestricted use                              No opt-out exceeding 30 days   No prospective-only deletion
