Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

This legislative summary and compliance brief (this \"Brief\") has been prepared by Whitfield Ames LLP (\"Whitfield Ames\" or \"we\") for Meridian Health Systems, Inc. (\"Meridian\") at the request of the Office of the General Counsel. The purpose of this Brief is to provide a comprehensive analysis of S.2314, the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (the \"Act\"), which was enacted by the New Jersey Legislature and signed into law with an effective date of March 1, 2025. This Brief addresses the Act\'s requirements with particular attention to their implications for Meridian as a covered healthcare entity that operates three hospital facilities within the State of New Jersey --- specifically, Meridian Atlantic Medical Center (Atlantic City), Meridian South Jersey Regional Hospital (Egg Harbor Township), and Meridian Princeton Medical Center (Plainsboro).

The Act imposes comprehensive obligations on healthcare entities that deploy artificial intelligence clinical decision-support tools in clinical care settings. These obligations span four principal domains: (1) algorithmic transparency and documentation requirements; (2) mandatory independent annual audits of deployed AI systems; (3) patient notification requirements when AI tools are used in clinical care decisions; and (4) affirmative vendor-contracting obligations that mandate inclusion of specific provisions in agreements between covered entities and AI vendors. Violations of the Act carry significant enforcement consequences, including civil penalties of up to fifty thousand dollars (\$50,000) per violation --- with each day of a continuing violation constituting a separate violation --- and the potential for the New Jersey Department of Health (\"NJ DOH\") to order the mandatory suspension of non-compliant AI systems pending remediation.

This Brief is of immediate practical relevance to Meridian because Meridian is currently in active negotiation with Cognitive Foundry, Inc. (\"Cognitive Foundry\") regarding the deployment of the ClinIQ™ Platform (version 4.2) (the \"ClinIQ™ Platform\" or \"ClinIQ™\") across Meridian\'s fourteen hospital facilities. Three of those facilities are located in the State of New Jersey and are therefore subject to the Act\'s requirements. Whitfield Ames serves as outside counsel to Meridian in the Cognitive Foundry transaction, and this Brief is intended to inform the ongoing negotiation of the Master Services Agreement (the \"Agreement\") between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry.

The central takeaway of this Brief is as follows: several positions currently advanced by Cognitive Foundry in the proposed Agreement --- particularly regarding audit access rights, model documentation transparency, and the scope of Meridian\'s contractual entitlements to information concerning the ClinIQ™ Platform --- are, in their present form, insufficient to enable Meridian to satisfy its statutory obligations under the Act. This is not merely a matter of commercial preference or risk allocation. The Act imposes mandatory requirements on covered entities that cannot be waived, conditioned, or circumvented through contractual arrangements with vendors. Accordingly, the regulatory backdrop created by the Act materially strengthens Meridian\'s negotiating posture on audit and transparency provisions, and Whitfield Ames recommends that Meridian present these requirements to Cognitive Foundry as a legal compliance baseline rather than as a preferred commercial position.

The sections that follow provide a detailed analysis of the Act\'s scope, requirements, enforcement mechanisms, and implications for the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement, together with specific recommendations for contract negotiation strategy and compliance implementation.

**[II. Scope of Coverage and Applicability to Meridian]{.underline}**

**A. Covered Entities**

The Act defines a \"covered entity\" broadly to encompass any healthcare facility, healthcare system, or provider organization that is licensed or operating within the State of New Jersey and that deploys, utilizes, or integrates AI-based tools in clinical decision-making processes. The definition is intentionally expansive. It is not limited to hospitals; it includes ambulatory surgical centers, long-term care facilities, outpatient clinics, and other provider organizations operating under New Jersey licensure. The critical nexus is the combination of (i) operation within New Jersey and (ii) deployment or utilization of AI tools in clinical decision-making at a New Jersey-based facility.

Meridian\'s status as a covered entity under the Act is clear and unambiguous. Meridian Health Systems, Inc. is a multi-state health system that operates fourteen hospital facilities across three states (Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey), including three hospitals located within the State of New Jersey:

1\. **Meridian Atlantic Medical Center** (Atlantic City, NJ)

2\. **Meridian South Jersey Regional Hospital** (Egg Harbor Township, NJ)

3\. **Meridian Princeton Medical Center** (Plainsboro, NJ)

Each of these three facilities is a licensed healthcare facility operating within the State of New Jersey. The planned deployment of the ClinIQ™ Platform at these facilities --- encompassing AI-driven sepsis prediction, radiology diagnostic support, and medication interaction analysis --- constitutes the use of AI in clinical decision-making within the meaning of the Act. Accordingly, each of Meridian\'s three New Jersey hospitals is independently a \"covered entity\" under the Act, and Meridian Health Systems, Inc., as the operating health system, bears the compliance obligations that attach to each covered facility.

It is worth noting that the Act\'s definition of \"covered entity\" applies at the facility level, meaning that compliance obligations attach independently to each New Jersey hospital. A violation at one facility does not automatically constitute a violation at another, but the Act permits NJ DOH to aggregate violations across facilities operated by the same health system for purposes of penalty assessment and enforcement action, which increases Meridian\'s aggregate exposure.

**B. Covered AI Systems**

The Act defines \"AI clinical decision-support tools\" (referred to in the statute as \"covered AI systems\") as systems employing machine learning, deep learning, natural language processing, or other algorithmic techniques to assist, inform, or generate clinical recommendations, diagnoses, treatment pathways, or other outputs that are used, or reasonably expected to be used, in the clinical care of patients. The definition is technology-neutral and encompasses both deterministic rule-based systems with machine-learning components and fully data-driven models. It covers systems that generate autonomous outputs as well as systems that provide recommendations subject to clinician review and override.

The ClinIQ™ Platform, as described in Cognitive Foundry\'s technical specifications and the draft Statement of Work (Exhibit A to the proposed Agreement), comprises three distinct modules:

> • **ClinIQ™ Sepsis Early Warning Module:** Employs a deep-learning model trained on electronic health record data to generate real-time sepsis risk scores and alert clinicians to patients at elevated risk of sepsis onset. The module integrates with the patient\'s EHR record and generates clinical alerts within the clinician workflow.
>
> • **ClinIQ™ Radiology Diagnostic Support Module:** Utilizes convolutional neural network architectures to analyze medical imaging data (including chest radiographs, CT scans, and MRI studies) and generate preliminary diagnostic impressions, flagging abnormalities for radiologist review.
>
> • **ClinIQ™ Medication Interaction Analysis Module:** Employs natural language processing and machine-learning techniques to analyze a patient\'s medication profile, clinical history, and genetic markers (where available) to identify potential drug-drug interactions, adverse reaction risks, and dosing optimization opportunities.

Each of these three modules falls squarely within the statutory definition of a covered AI system. Each employs machine learning or deep learning techniques. Each generates outputs --- risk scores, diagnostic impressions, interaction alerts --- that are used or reasonably expected to be used in the clinical care of patients at Meridian\'s facilities. The planned integration of ClinIQ™ with Meridian\'s Epic EHR instance (\"MeridianConnect\") as a clinical workflow tool further confirms the covered status of these modules, as the integration is specifically designed to embed AI-generated outputs into the clinical decision-making workflow used by Meridian\'s clinicians at the point of care.

There is no credible argument that any of the three ClinIQ™ modules falls outside the scope of the Act. Cognitive Foundry\'s product literature, FDA regulatory correspondence, and technical documentation all describe ClinIQ™ as a clinical decision-support tool designed to assist clinicians in making diagnostic and treatment decisions. The Act\'s definition is precisely calibrated to cover tools of this nature.

**C. Geographic Application**

The Act\'s jurisdictional scope is defined by the location of the clinical decision-making, not by the location of the vendor, the AI vendor\'s corporate headquarters, or the physical infrastructure on which the AI system operates. Specifically, the Act applies to any covered AI system that is used in clinical decision-making at a facility located within the State of New Jersey, regardless of where the vendor is incorporated, where the model was developed or trained, or where the computational infrastructure supporting the AI system is physically situated.

This geographic scope provision is directly relevant to the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry transaction. Cognitive Foundry is incorporated in the State of Delaware and maintains its principal offices in Menlo Park, California. The ClinIQ™ Platform is hosted on Amazon Web Services GovCloud infrastructure in the us-east-1 region (Northern Virginia). Neither Cognitive Foundry\'s corporate domicile nor the physical location of the AWS GovCloud servers is within the State of New Jersey. However, because the ClinIQ™ Platform will be used to generate clinical decision-support outputs for patients receiving care at Meridian\'s three New Jersey hospitals, the Act\'s requirements apply in full to the deployment of ClinIQ™ at those facilities.

Cognitive Foundry should not be expected to dispute this point, as the geographic application provision is clear on its face. However, to the extent that Cognitive Foundry seeks to argue that its obligations under the Agreement should be limited because its infrastructure and operations are located outside New Jersey, Meridian should be prepared to respond that the Act\'s coverage is triggered by the use of AI tools at New Jersey facilities, not by the location of the vendor\'s operations.

**[III. Key Requirements of the Act]{.underline}**

**A. Algorithmic Transparency**

The Act imposes on covered entities a core obligation to ensure algorithmic transparency for all AI systems used in clinical decision-making at their New Jersey facilities. This transparency obligation is both a substantive compliance requirement and a predicate for several of the Act\'s other mandates, including the annual audit requirement and the vendor contracting obligations discussed below.

The Act specifies four categories of transparency documentation that covered entities must ensure are created, maintained, and made accessible:

**1. Training Data Documentation.** Covered entities must ensure that documentation is maintained regarding the model\'s training data sources, including the provenance of the data (i.e., the institutional, geographic, and temporal sources from which training data was derived), the demographic composition of the training data (including representation across racial, ethnic, sex, age, socioeconomic, language, and disability-status categories), and an assessment of the training data\'s representativeness relative to the patient populations served by the covered entity\'s facilities. This requirement is designed to enable identification of potential biases arising from unrepresentative training data --- a concern that has been extensively documented in the AI-in-healthcare literature and that is particularly acute for health systems, like Meridian, that serve demographically diverse patient populations.

**2. Validation Methodology.** Covered entities must ensure documentation of the validation methodology employed for each covered AI system, including identification of internal and external validation datasets, the performance metrics used to evaluate the system\'s accuracy and reliability (e.g., sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, and calibration metrics), and the statistical methods employed in validation analyses. The Act requires that validation documentation be sufficiently detailed to permit an independent reviewer to assess the rigor and adequacy of the validation process.

**3. Known Limitations.** Covered entities must maintain documentation of the known limitations of each covered AI system, including identified failure modes, edge cases in which the system\'s performance may be unreliable, and population subgroups for which the system\'s performance may be degraded relative to the general patient population. This requirement reflects the legislative recognition that AI clinical decision-support tools are not infallible and that clinicians and healthcare administrators must be informed of the conditions under which an AI system\'s outputs should be treated with heightened scrutiny or skepticism.

**4. Bias Testing Results.** Covered entities must maintain documentation of bias testing results for each covered AI system. Bias testing must include analyses of differential performance across protected demographic categories, specifically including race, ethnicity, sex, age, socioeconomic status, language, and disability status. The Act does not prescribe a specific bias-testing methodology but requires that the testing be sufficiently rigorous to identify material disparities in system performance across the enumerated demographic categories.

With respect to the form and availability of transparency documentation, the Act establishes a tiered access framework. Transparency documentation must be maintained in a form that is accessible to (i) clinical leadership personnel at the covered entity\'s facilities, who are expected to use the documentation to inform clinical governance decisions regarding the AI system\'s deployment and use; (ii) the NJ Department of Health, which may request production of transparency documentation in connection with regulatory oversight, investigation, or enforcement; and (iii) patients, who are entitled to receive a summarized version of the transparency documentation upon request. The patient-facing summary must be written in plain language and must address, at a minimum, the general nature of the AI system, the types of data on which it was trained, its known limitations, and the fact that its outputs are subject to clinician review.

The transparency obligation is ongoing. It is not satisfied by a one-time documentation exercise at the time of initial deployment. Covered entities must ensure that transparency documentation is updated to reflect material changes to the AI system, including model updates, retraining on new data, changes in performance metrics, and newly identified limitations or failure modes.

**B. Mandatory Independent Annual Audit**

The Act requires that covered entities conduct or commission an independent annual audit of each AI system used in clinical decision-making at their New Jersey facilities. This audit requirement is one of the Act\'s most operationally significant provisions and has direct implications for the structure of the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement.

**Independence Requirement.** The Act defines \"independent\" to mean that the audit must be performed by an auditor that has no financial interest in the AI vendor and no role in the development, marketing, or deployment of the AI system under review. The independence requirement is designed to ensure the credibility and objectivity of the audit process. An audit conducted by the vendor itself, by a firm with an equity stake in the vendor, or by personnel who participated in the development of the AI system would not satisfy the independence requirement. The Act does not require that the auditor be a government entity or a specific type of organization; it permits the covered entity to engage a qualified private-sector auditor, provided that the independence criteria are met.

**Minimum Audit Scope.** The Act specifies a minimum scope for the required annual audit, which includes the following elements:

> \(a\) Review of model architecture documentation and training data provenance, to verify that the AI system\'s design and data inputs are consistent with the transparency documentation maintained by the covered entity;
>
> \(b\) Assessment of validation results and ongoing performance metrics, to evaluate whether the AI system continues to perform at the level of accuracy and reliability documented at the time of its deployment;
>
> \(c\) Evaluation of bias and fairness across demographic groups, to determine whether the AI system exhibits differential performance across the protected demographic categories specified by the Act;
>
> \(d\) Review of incident reports and adverse-event correlation data, to identify any instances in which the AI system\'s outputs may have contributed to or been associated with adverse patient outcomes; and
>
> \(e\) Assessment of data governance practices, including the handling, storage, processing, and protection of protected health information (\"PHI\") in connection with the AI system\'s operation.

These five elements constitute the statutory minimum. Covered entities are free to expand the scope of the audit beyond these elements, and in many cases prudent risk management will counsel doing so. However, an audit that fails to address any one of these five elements will not satisfy the Act\'s requirements.

**Reporting and Production Requirements.** Audit reports must be completed within ninety (90) days of the close of the audit period. The audit period is defined as the twelve-month period preceding the audit. Completed audit reports must be retained by the covered entity and must be made available to the NJ Department of Health upon request. The Act requires that covered entities produce audit reports to NJ DOH within fifteen (15) business days of receiving a request. NJ DOH may request audit reports at any time and is not required to demonstrate cause or provide advance notice beyond the request itself.

**Audit Cycle.** The first mandatory audit must be completed within twelve (12) months of the AI system\'s initial deployment in clinical use at a covered facility. Subsequent audits must be conducted annually thereafter. The Act does not specify a particular calendar date for audit completion; the cycle is measured from the date of initial deployment.

**Relevance to Meridian\'s Timeline.** Assuming the current Target Go-Live date of January 6, 2026, the first mandatory audit of ClinIQ™ under the Act would need to be completed no later than January 6, 2027. This means that Meridian must have an independent auditor engaged, audit access rights contractually secured, and the audit process initiated no later than the fourth quarter of 2026 to ensure timely completion of the audit report within the ninety-day reporting window. Working backward from the January 6, 2027 deadline, and accounting for the ninety-day report completion requirement, the audit fieldwork should be substantially complete by approximately October 2026.

**C. Patient Notification**

The Act requires that covered entities inform patients when AI tools are used in their clinical care decisions. This patient notification obligation reflects the Act\'s overarching policy of transparency and informed consent in the use of AI-assisted healthcare.

**Timing.** The notification must be provided in a timely manner --- specifically, prior to or concurrent with the clinical encounter in which the AI tool is applied. The Act does not require that notification be provided days or weeks in advance of the encounter; however, it must be provided at a point in time that gives the patient a meaningful opportunity to be aware that AI tools are being used in their care. Notification that is provided only after the clinical encounter has concluded would not satisfy this requirement.

**Content.** The Act specifies four minimum content elements for patient notifications:

> \(a\) Identification that an AI system is being used in connection with the patient\'s clinical care;
>
> \(b\) A general description of the AI system\'s function --- i.e., what the AI system does and how it is used in the clinical workflow (e.g., \"An AI system is used to analyze your medical images and flag potential abnormalities for your radiologist\'s review\");
>
> \(c\) A statement that the AI output is reviewed by a licensed clinician and that the AI system does not make autonomous clinical decisions; and
>
> \(d\) Information on how the patient may request additional information about the AI system, including the summarized transparency documentation described in Section III.A above.

**Delivery Methods.** The Act is intentionally flexible regarding the mechanism of notification delivery. Permissible methods include incorporation into intake forms (paper or electronic), display through electronic health record patient portals (such as Meridian\'s MyMeridian patient portal), posted notices in clinical areas, and direct communication by the treating clinician. The Act does not mandate a single delivery mechanism, and covered entities may use a combination of methods to ensure that patients are effectively notified. The key requirement is that the notification be clear, in plain language, and actually provided to the patient --- not merely available upon request.

**Language Access.** While the Act does not include a separate language-access provision, its requirement that notifications be in \"plain language\" and provided in a \"clear\" manner should be read in conjunction with New Jersey\'s existing language-access requirements for healthcare facilities, which require that critical patient communications be available in languages spoken by significant portions of the facility\'s patient population. Meridian\'s compliance team should evaluate whether ClinIQ™-related patient notifications need to be translated into Spanish, Portuguese, Chinese, Korean, or other languages commonly spoken by patients at Meridian\'s three New Jersey facilities.

**Operational Implications for Meridian.** Patient notification processes must be fully operationalized across all three New Jersey hospitals before the Target Go-Live date of January 6, 2026. This will require coordination among multiple Meridian departments and stakeholders, including the Office of the General Counsel, the Compliance Department, the Patient Experience team, clinical operations leadership at each NJ facility, and the Health Information Technology team responsible for MeridianConnect (Epic) and the MyMeridian patient portal. Whitfield Ames recommends that Meridian convene a cross-functional working group to design, test, and implement the patient notification workflow no later than the third quarter of 2025, to allow adequate time for development, review, and training before Go-Live.

**D. Vendor Contractual Requirements**

One of the most consequential features of the Act, and the provision with the most direct implications for the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement, is the Act\'s imposition of affirmative obligations on covered entities regarding the content of their contracts with AI vendors. The Act does not merely regulate the deployment of AI systems at the facility level; it reaches backward into the vendor relationship and mandates that certain substantive provisions be included in the contracts that govern the covered entity\'s access to AI systems and related documentation.

The Act requires that contracts between covered entities and AI vendors include, at a minimum, the following provisions:

**1. Model Documentation Access Rights.** The contract must provide the covered entity with a right to access sufficient model documentation to fulfill its transparency obligations under the Act. This includes, but is not limited to, documentation of training data provenance, validation methodology, known limitations, and bias testing results. The right of access must be affirmative and non-discretionary --- i.e., the covered entity must be able to obtain this documentation as a matter of contractual right, not merely upon the vendor\'s consent or at the vendor\'s discretion.

**2. Independent Audit Rights.** The contract must provide the covered entity with a right to conduct or commission independent audits of the AI system and to access model documentation necessary for the auditor to complete the statutory audit scope described in Section III.B above. This provision is critical because the covered entity\'s ability to satisfy the annual audit requirement depends entirely on its contractual ability to compel the vendor to provide the auditor with access to the information and systems necessary to conduct the audit.

**3. Data Governance Terms.** The contract must include data governance terms specifying how the vendor will handle, store, process, and return or destroy PHI in compliance with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (\"HIPAA\") and applicable state law. While this requirement overlaps with existing HIPAA Business Associate Agreement obligations, the Act creates an independent state-law mandate that data governance terms be specifically addressed in the vendor contract, and NJ DOH may review these terms as part of its oversight of covered AI systems.

**4. Regulatory Cooperation.** The contract must include provisions addressing the vendor\'s obligation to cooperate with the NJ Department of Health in connection with any inquiry or investigation related to the AI system. The Act recognizes that covered entities may be unable to respond fully to NJ DOH inquiries without the vendor\'s cooperation, particularly with respect to technical aspects of the AI system\'s design, training, and performance.

**Prohibition on Waiver.** The Act includes an anti-waiver provision that prohibits contractual terms that waive, limit, or condition the covered entity\'s rights under the Act in a manner that would prevent the entity from satisfying its statutory obligations. This provision is particularly significant because it means that a vendor cannot, through superior bargaining power or restrictive intellectual property provisions, contractually prevent a covered entity from accessing the documentation and audit rights necessary for compliance. Any such contractual restriction is, as a matter of New Jersey law, unenforceable to the extent it conflicts with the Act\'s requirements.

**Critical Implication for the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement.** The Master Services Agreement between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry must, as a matter of New Jersey law, include provisions that give Meridian access to sufficient model documentation and audit cooperation to fulfill its obligations under the Act. Contractual terms that restrict Meridian\'s audit rights or documentation access to a degree that prevents compliance with the Act are not merely a commercial disadvantage or a suboptimal negotiation outcome --- they create direct regulatory exposure for Meridian, including potential civil penalties and mandatory suspension of the ClinIQ™ Platform at Meridian\'s three New Jersey hospitals. Whitfield Ames cannot advise Meridian to execute an Agreement that fails to include these provisions.

**E. Enforcement.** The NJ Department of Health is designated as the primary enforcement authority under the Act. The Act grants NJ DOH a comprehensive suite of enforcement powers, which are summarized below.

**Civil Penalties.** NJ DOH may impose civil penalties of up to fifty thousand dollars (\$50,000) per violation. Each day of a continuing violation constitutes a separate violation for purposes of penalty calculation. This \"per day\" accrual mechanism means that a covered entity that fails to complete a required annual audit, for example, could face accumulating penalties for each day that the audit remains overdue, potentially resulting in aggregate penalties that are orders of magnitude greater than the per-violation cap.

**Mandatory Suspension.** NJ DOH has the authority to require the suspension of non-compliant AI systems pending remediation. A suspension order would require Meridian to cease using the affected ClinIQ™ module(s) at its New Jersey facilities until the identified compliance deficiency is cured. A suspension order would have significant operational and clinical implications, particularly if it affects the sepsis early warning module, which is expected to become integrated into Meridian\'s standard-of-care clinical workflow.

**Document Production.** NJ DOH has the authority to require covered entities to produce audit reports, transparency documentation, and vendor contracts for inspection. As noted above, covered entities must produce audit reports within fifteen (15) business days of a DOH request.

**Site Inspections and Interviews.** NJ DOH has the authority to conduct site inspections of covered facilities and to interview clinical and administrative personnel regarding the deployment, use, and governance of covered AI systems. These inspections may be conducted with or without advance notice, consistent with NJ DOH\'s general inspection authority over licensed healthcare facilities.

**No Private Right of Action.** The Act does not create a private right of action. Patients and other individuals cannot bring suit directly under the Act to enforce its provisions or to recover damages for violations. However, the Act expressly provides that it does not preempt or limit existing state or federal causes of action, including claims for medical malpractice, negligence, products liability, or violations of the New Jersey Consumer Fraud Act. This means that while patients cannot sue under the Act itself, evidence of non-compliance with the Act\'s requirements could be relevant and potentially admissible in other litigation --- for example, in a medical malpractice action alleging that an AI system contributed to a misdiagnosis or a failure to diagnose.

**Penalties Assessed Against the Covered Entity.** A critical structural feature of the Act\'s enforcement framework is that civil penalties and enforcement actions are directed against the covered entity --- i.e., Meridian --- not against the AI vendor. Cognitive Foundry is not a \"covered entity\" under the Act and is not directly subject to its penalty provisions. This allocation of enforcement exposure underscores the importance of robust contractual risk allocation and indemnification provisions in the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement. If Meridian incurs penalties or is subject to enforcement action because Cognitive Foundry failed to provide required documentation, audit access, or cooperation, Meridian\'s only recourse against Cognitive Foundry will be contractual. The Agreement must therefore include indemnification provisions that adequately address this risk.

**[IV. Implications for the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement]{.underline}**

The Act\'s requirements have direct, specific, and in several instances dispositive implications for the terms of the proposed Master Services Agreement between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry. This section identifies the key areas of impact and provides recommendations for contract provisions necessary to achieve compliance.

**A. Audit Rights --- Current Vendor Position vs. Statutory Requirements**

Cognitive Foundry\'s current position in the proposed Agreement, as reflected in Article 10 (AI Model Transparency and Audit) of the most recent draft circulated by Cognitive Foundry\'s counsel on June 6, 2025, limits Meridian\'s audit access in several material respects. Specifically, the current draft:

> • Restricts Meridian\'s audit rights to financial audits and SOC 2 report reviews, without providing for independent audits of the AI model\'s clinical performance, bias, or training data provenance;
>
> • Does not expressly provide for independent third-party audit rights at the scope required by the Act, limiting audit access to \"mutually agreed\" auditors with Cognitive Foundry retaining a right of approval that could be exercised to exclude auditors whose scope of review Cognitive Foundry deems intrusive;
>
> • Conditions any model-level audit access on Cognitive Foundry\'s \"prior written consent, not to be unreasonably withheld,\" which, while nominally a reasonableness standard, introduces discretion that is inconsistent with the Act\'s mandatory audit requirement.

The Act requires that Meridian have an unconditional contractual right to commission an independent annual audit with access to, at minimum, model architecture documentation, training data provenance, validation results, and the ability to conduct test queries against the model. This corresponds to at least Langford Tier 2 access (described in detail in Appendix C).

In the course of the parties\' negotiation in June 2025, Cognitive Foundry\'s CEO, Tomás Eriksson, and Chief Technology Officer signaled flexibility on the concept of a qualified third-party audit, acknowledging that regulatory requirements may necessitate audit access beyond what Cognitive Foundry\'s standard contract template provides. However, the Vendor CTO identified access to raw model weights as a \"hard no,\" citing trade secret protection concerns and the risk that extracted model weights could be used to replicate or reverse-engineer the ClinIQ™ Platform.

Whitfield Ames has evaluated this concern in light of the Act\'s requirements and the available audit frameworks. Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC (\"Langford\"), led by Dr. Anil Cherian, Managing Director, has been pre-approved by both Meridian and Cognitive Foundry as an acceptable independent auditor. Langford offers a tiered audit access framework that is well-suited to balancing Meridian\'s regulatory compliance needs with Cognitive Foundry\'s intellectual property concerns:

> • **Langford Tier 2 Access** (model architecture documentation, training data provenance, validation results, test queries) is likely sufficient for most annual regulatory compliance audits under the Act. Tier 2 access does not require disclosure of raw model weights or proprietary source code.
>
> • **Langford Tier 3 Access** (model weights and embeddings reviewed in a secure enclave environment, with no data extraction permitted) should be reserved for cause-based investigations or regulatory inquiries --- for example, where NJ DOH has initiated an investigation and requires a deeper level of technical review than Tier 2 provides.

The distinction between Tier 2 and Tier 3 is important because it addresses the Vendor CTO\'s \"hard no\" on raw model weight access. Tier 3 does not provide extractable access to model weights; rather, it permits the auditor to review model weights and embeddings within a secure computational enclave maintained by Langford, with strict prohibitions on data extraction, copying, or retention. This enclave protocol is specifically designed to permit technical review without creating a risk of model replication or reverse engineering.

**Recommendation:** The Agreement must include a mandatory annual independent audit right at no less than Langford Tier 2 access, exercisable by Meridian without Cognitive Foundry\'s prior consent (subject to reasonable advance notice and scheduling coordination). The Agreement should also include an escalation pathway to Langford Tier 3 access for cause-based or regulatory-compelled audits --- specifically, audits initiated in response to (i) a NJ DOH inquiry or investigation, (ii) a material adverse event or pattern of adverse events potentially associated with ClinIQ™, or (iii) other circumstances reasonably requiring a deeper level of technical review. Langford\'s confidentiality protections --- including a separate NDA with Cognitive Foundry, secure-environment-only access, strict prohibitions on data extraction, and mandatory destruction of all work product after delivery to Meridian --- adequately address Cognitive Foundry\'s trade-secret concerns and should be memorialized in an exhibit or schedule to the Agreement.

**B. Model Transparency --- Current Vendor Position vs. Statutory Requirements**

Cognitive Foundry\'s current position in the proposed Agreement, as reflected in Article 6 (Intellectual Property Ownership) and Article 10 (AI Model Transparency and Audit), treats all model intellectual property --- including model weights, model architecture, fine-tuned adaptations, and related documentation --- as exclusive Vendor property. Under the current draft, Meridian receives only an output license, which grants Meridian the right to use the outputs generated by the ClinIQ™ Platform but does not grant Meridian any rights in or access to the underlying model or its documentation, except to the extent Cognitive Foundry elects, in its sole discretion, to provide such access.

The Act does not require that Meridian own model weights, model architecture, or any other component of the ClinIQ™ Platform\'s intellectual property. Whitfield Ames is not recommending that Meridian seek to acquire ownership of Cognitive Foundry\'s model IP. However, the Act does require that Meridian be able to access and, if necessary, produce to NJ DOH documentation of training data sources, validation methodology, known limitations, and bias testing results. The Act further requires that this access be secured contractually --- i.e., that the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement include provisions granting Meridian the right to obtain this documentation.

Cognitive Foundry\'s current position --- that all model documentation access is discretionary and subject to Vendor consent --- is inconsistent with the Act\'s mandate that covered entities contractually secure access rights sufficient to fulfill their transparency obligations. A provision that conditions Meridian\'s access to required documentation on Cognitive Foundry\'s discretionary consent would, by its terms, permit Cognitive Foundry to withhold documentation that Meridian is legally required to maintain and produce. This is precisely the type of contractual arrangement that the Act\'s anti-waiver provision is designed to prohibit.

**Recommendation:** The Agreement must include an affirmative, non-discretionary right for Meridian to obtain and maintain model documentation sufficient for compliance with the Act\'s transparency requirements. This right is distinct from the intellectual property ownership question and should be negotiated as a separate, compliance-driven provision. Specifically, the Agreement should require Cognitive Foundry to provide Meridian, on an initial and ongoing basis, with the following documentation:

> \(a\) Documentation of the training data sources used to develop and fine-tune each ClinIQ™ module, including data provenance, demographic composition, and representativeness analysis;
>
> \(b\) Documentation of the validation methodology employed for each ClinIQ™ module, including identification of validation datasets, performance metrics, and statistical methods;
>
> \(c\) Documentation of known limitations, including identified failure modes, edge cases, and population subgroups for which performance may be degraded; and
>
> \(d\) Documentation of bias testing results, including analyses of differential performance across race, ethnicity, sex, age, socioeconomic status, language, and disability status.

This documentation should be updated by Cognitive Foundry following any material change to the ClinIQ™ Platform, including model updates, retraining, or changes to training data, and should be provided to Meridian within a reasonable timeframe (e.g., thirty days) following such changes.

**C. Regulatory Cooperation**

The Act contemplates that NJ DOH may require production of audit reports and may initiate inquiries or investigations concerning deployed AI systems at covered facilities. As noted in Section III.E above, NJ DOH has broad authority to request documentation, conduct site inspections, and interview personnel in connection with its oversight of covered AI systems.

Meridian\'s ability to respond to NJ DOH inquiries will, in many instances, depend on Cognitive Foundry\'s cooperation. Technical questions regarding model architecture, training data, validation methodology, and system performance are within Cognitive Foundry\'s domain of knowledge, and Meridian cannot be expected to respond to NJ DOH inquiries on these subjects without Cognitive Foundry\'s assistance.

**Recommendation:** The Agreement should include an express obligation on Cognitive Foundry to cooperate with Meridian in responding to NJ DOH requests related to ClinIQ™. This obligation should include:

> \(a\) Making qualified Cognitive Foundry personnel available for interviews by NJ DOH upon reasonable notice;
>
> \(b\) Producing additional documentation requested by NJ DOH, through Meridian, within timeframes consistent with the Act\'s fifteen (15) business-day production requirement;
>
> \(c\) Providing technical assistance to Meridian in preparing responses to NJ DOH inquiries; and
>
> \(d\) Promptly notifying Meridian of any direct communications from NJ DOH or any other regulatory authority concerning the ClinIQ™ Platform.

**D. Patient Notification Support**

While the Act\'s patient notification obligation falls on the covered entity --- i.e., Meridian --- rather than on the AI vendor, effective patient notification requires accurate and current information about the AI system\'s functionality, limitations, and the nature of clinician review and oversight. Meridian\'s compliance with the patient notification requirement will depend in part on the quality and accuracy of the information provided by Cognitive Foundry about the ClinIQ™ Platform.

**Recommendation:** The Agreement should require Cognitive Foundry to provide Meridian with accurate, current descriptions of ClinIQ™ functionality, known limitations, and the nature and extent of clinician-review protocols, in a form suitable for incorporation into patient-facing disclosure materials. This information should be updated by Cognitive Foundry whenever material changes are made to the ClinIQ™ Platform, and Cognitive Foundry should review and approve the accuracy of any patient-facing descriptions derived from vendor-provided materials. The obligation to review and approve patient-facing descriptions should be subject to a reasonable turnaround time (e.g., ten business days) to avoid delaying Meridian\'s compliance efforts.

**E. Indemnification for Regulatory Penalties**

As discussed in Section III.E above, civil penalties under the Act are assessed against Meridian as the covered entity. Cognitive Foundry is not directly subject to the Act\'s penalty provisions. If Meridian incurs penalties because Cognitive Foundry failed to provide required documentation, audit access, or regulatory cooperation --- i.e., if Meridian\'s non-compliance is attributable to Cognitive Foundry\'s breach of its contractual obligations --- Meridian\'s only recourse against Cognitive Foundry will be contractual.

**Recommendation:** The Agreement\'s indemnification provisions should expressly address the allocation of penalty exposure arising from Cognitive Foundry\'s failure to provide contractually required documentation, audit access, or cooperation that results in a violation of the Act. Specifically:

> \(a\) Cognitive Foundry should indemnify Meridian for civil penalties assessed under the Act to the extent such penalties arise from or are attributable to Cognitive Foundry\'s breach of its documentation, audit, or cooperation obligations under the Agreement;
>
> \(b\) The scope of this indemnification should be clearly defined and should not be subject to the general liability cap applicable to other categories of damages under the Agreement, or, in the alternative, should be subject to a separate, higher cap commensurate with the potential magnitude of penalty exposure (given the per-day accrual mechanism, aggregate penalties could be substantial); and
>
> \(c\) The Agreement should include a clear delineation between penalties attributable to Vendor non-compliance (indemnifiable) and penalties attributable to Meridian\'s own operational failures (not indemnifiable by Vendor), to avoid ambiguity in enforcement scenarios.

The scope of Vendor indemnification for regulatory penalties, and the treatment of such penalties within or outside the general liability cap, are identified as open negotiation items on the Contested Terms Matrix finalized July 1, 2025. The regulatory penalty framework established by the Act provides a strong basis for Meridian to argue that regulatory penalty indemnification should be carved out from the general liability cap, and Whitfield Ames recommends that Meridian advance this position in the upcoming negotiation session.

**F. Data Governance and PHI**

The Act requires that vendor contracts include data governance terms specifying how the vendor will handle, store, process, and return or destroy PHI in compliance with HIPAA and applicable state law. These provisions should be coordinated with the Business Associate Agreement between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry (Exhibit F to the proposed Agreement, executed June 2, 2025) to ensure consistency and to avoid conflicting obligations.

Particular attention should be paid to Cognitive Foundry\'s proposed use of aggregated and de-identified data derived from Meridian\'s patient records. The Act\'s transparency requirements extend to how training data is sourced and used, including whether the vendor uses data from one covered entity to train or refine models deployed at other covered entities. Cognitive Foundry\'s draft Agreement includes a provision (Section 5.4) granting Cognitive Foundry the right to use \"aggregated, de-identified data\" derived from Meridian\'s use of ClinIQ™ to \"improve, enhance, and refine\" the ClinIQ™ Platform. This provision should be evaluated in light of the Act\'s transparency and documentation mandates, which require Meridian to maintain documentation of training data sources and provenance. If Cognitive Foundry uses Meridian-derived data to retrain the model, this retraining must be documented, and Meridian must have access to updated transparency documentation reflecting the changed training data composition.

**Recommendation:** Meridian should condition any consent to Cognitive Foundry\'s use of aggregated or de-identified data on Cognitive Foundry\'s agreement to (i) document such use in the transparency materials provided to Meridian, (ii) update training data provenance documentation to reflect any retraining that incorporates Meridian-derived data, and (iii) ensure that de-identification standards meet the requirements of HIPAA\'s Safe Harbor or Expert Determination methods, as well as any additional de-identification standards imposed by New Jersey state law.

**[V. Negotiation Strategy Implications]{.underline}**

The Act\'s mandatory requirements are non-negotiable as a matter of New Jersey law. Any contractual provision in the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement that would prevent Meridian from complying with the Act\'s transparency, audit, patient notification, or vendor-contracting requirements exposes Meridian to civil penalties of up to fifty thousand dollars (\$50,000) per violation per day, potential mandatory suspension of the ClinIQ™ Platform at three New Jersey hospitals, and reputational harm associated with regulatory enforcement actions. These consequences are not hypothetical; the Act has been in effect since March 1, 2025, and NJ DOH has publicly indicated its intention to actively enforce the Act\'s provisions beginning in the first quarter of 2026, which would coincide with Meridian\'s anticipated Go-Live timeline.

This regulatory backdrop materially strengthens Meridian\'s negotiating position on audit rights and model transparency terms. These items have been identified by Meridian\'s internal negotiation team as the second-highest negotiation priority (after pricing and payment terms), and the Act provides a compelling, objective basis for Meridian to insist on terms that Cognitive Foundry has resisted in prior negotiation rounds.

Whitfield Ames recommends that Meridian adopt the following strategic approach for the upcoming negotiation sessions:

**1. Reframe the Discussion.** Meridian should present the Act\'s requirements to Cognitive Foundry not as Meridian\'s preferred commercial position but as a legal compliance baseline that any contract must satisfy. This reframing is important because it shifts the audit-rights and transparency discussions from a \"Customer preference\" framework --- in which Cognitive Foundry can position itself as making voluntary concessions --- to a \"regulatory mandate\" framework, in which both parties must achieve a contractual structure that satisfies applicable law. Whitfield Ames recommends that this Brief, or a redacted summary suitable for sharing with Cognitive Foundry, be provided to Cognitive Foundry\'s legal counsel as part of Meridian\'s compromise proposal to demonstrate the legal basis for Meridian\'s positions.

**2. Leverage the Timeline.** Compromise proposals are due July 14, 2025, and the joint negotiation session is scheduled for July 18, 2025. This Brief should be shared with Meridian\'s negotiating team --- including Dr. Narayanan, Mr. Delgado, and any other members of the negotiation team --- in advance of the compromise-proposal deadline to inform the drafting of Meridian\'s audit-rights and transparency proposals. The regulatory analysis contained herein should be incorporated into Meridian\'s compromise proposal as a legal basis for its requested terms.

**3. Consider Enterprise-Wide Application.** The three New Jersey facilities represent a subset of Meridian\'s fourteen covered facilities. However, Meridian may wish to consider extending Act-level audit and transparency protections across all fourteen facilities as a matter of operational consistency and enterprise risk management. Operating under two different audit-rights frameworks --- one for New Jersey facilities subject to the Act and one for non-New Jersey facilities --- would create administrative complexity, increase the risk of compliance gaps, and potentially create an inference that Meridian is not committed to the same level of AI governance at its non-New Jersey facilities. Moreover, as discussed in Section VII below, similar legislation is pending or anticipated in other states where Meridian operates, including Delaware and Pennsylvania. Structuring the Agreement to provide uniform audit and transparency rights across all facilities would position Meridian for compliance with anticipated future legislation without requiring contract amendments.

**4. Maintain Flexibility on Implementation Details.** While the Act\'s substantive requirements are non-negotiable, the manner in which those requirements are implemented in the Agreement offers room for creative solutions that address Cognitive Foundry\'s legitimate intellectual property concerns. The Langford tiered audit framework, the use of secure enclaves for model weight review, and the NDA-protected access protocols are examples of implementation mechanisms that satisfy Meridian\'s regulatory obligations while providing Cognitive Foundry with meaningful protections against misuse of its proprietary technology. Meridian should signal willingness to work collaboratively on implementation details, even as it maintains firmness on the substantive rights that the Act requires.

Meridian\'s Compliance Department should maintain a compliance calendar tracking each of these obligations and deadlines, with responsibility assignments for each item.

**[VII. Recommendations]{.underline}**

Based on the foregoing analysis, Whitfield Ames makes the following recommendations to Meridian:

**1. Contractual Provisions for Compliance.** The following provisions must be included in the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Master Services Agreement in order to achieve compliance with the Act:

> \(a\) An affirmative, non-discretionary right for Meridian to obtain and maintain transparency documentation sufficient to fulfill the Act\'s algorithmic transparency requirements, including documentation of training data sources, validation methodology, known limitations, and bias testing results for each ClinIQ™ module;
>
> \(b\) A mandatory annual independent audit right at no less than Langford Tier 2 access, exercisable by Meridian without Cognitive Foundry\'s prior consent, with an escalation pathway to Langford Tier 3 access for cause-based or regulatory-compelled audits;
>
> \(c\) Langford confidentiality protections (NDA with Cognitive Foundry, secure-environment-only access, no data extraction, mandatory work product destruction) memorialized in an exhibit or schedule to the Agreement;
>
> \(d\) Data governance provisions specifying Cognitive Foundry\'s obligations regarding the handling, storage, processing, and return or destruction of PHI, coordinated with the executed Business Associate Agreement (Exhibit F);
>
> \(e\) An express obligation on Cognitive Foundry to cooperate with Meridian in responding to NJ DOH inquiries and investigations, including making personnel available, producing documentation within statutory timeframes, and providing technical assistance;
>
> \(f\) An obligation on Cognitive Foundry to provide accurate, current descriptions of ClinIQ™ functionality, limitations, and clinician-review protocols in a form suitable for patient-facing notification materials;
>
> \(g\) Indemnification provisions addressing Cognitive Foundry\'s liability for civil penalties assessed against Meridian under the Act to the extent arising from Cognitive Foundry\'s breach of its documentation, audit, or cooperation obligations, with such indemnification carved out from the general liability cap or subject to a separate, higher cap; and
>
> \(h\) An anti-waiver provision confirming that no term of the Agreement shall be construed to waive, limit, or condition Meridian\'s rights or obligations under the Act or any comparable state law.

**2. Cross-Functional Implementation.** Meridian should convene a cross-functional working group comprising representatives of the Office of the General Counsel, the Compliance Department, Clinical Operations (with representation from each NJ facility), the Patient Experience team, and the Health Information Technology team to design, test, and implement the patient notification workflow and transparency documentation management processes. This working group should be convened no later than August 2025 to allow adequate time for development, review, training, and testing before the January 6, 2026 Target Go-Live.

**3. Langford Engagement.** Meridian should initiate engagement discussions with Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC, with the objective of executing an audit engagement letter for the first annual audit of ClinIQ™. Audit engagement terms should be negotiated in parallel with the Master Services Agreement to ensure that the audit engagement is consistent with the contractual audit-rights provisions. Dr. Anil Cherian has been pre-approved by both Meridian and Cognitive Foundry, and early engagement will facilitate timely completion of the first audit within the statutory deadline.

**4. Board Risk Committee Briefing.** Meridian\'s Board Risk Committee has designated AI-related liability as a Tier 1 enterprise risk. Whitfield Ames recommends that the Board Risk Committee be briefed on the Act\'s requirements and their treatment in the proposed Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement. The briefing should address the scope of Meridian\'s regulatory exposure under the Act, the contractual provisions being negotiated to mitigate that exposure, the status of the Langford audit engagement, and the operational readiness of Meridian\'s NJ facilities for patient notification and transparency compliance. A briefing memorandum can be prepared by Whitfield Ames in coordination with the Office of the General Counsel.

**5. Multi-State Legislative Monitoring.** While the Act currently applies only to Meridian\'s three New Jersey facilities, similar legislation is pending or anticipated in at least two other states where Meridian operates hospital facilities: Delaware (H.B. 412, introduced April 2025, addressing AI transparency in healthcare settings) and Pennsylvania (draft legislation circulated by the Pennsylvania Health Committee in May 2025, modeled in part on the New Jersey Act). Meridian should structure the Agreement to accommodate the expansion of compliance obligations across all fourteen covered facilities as additional states enact comparable legislation. Whitfield Ames recommends including a \"regulatory expansion\" clause in the Agreement providing that Cognitive Foundry\'s documentation, audit, and cooperation obligations shall extend to any Meridian facility at which the use of ClinIQ™ becomes subject to comparable state or federal transparency and audit requirements, without the need for a contract amendment.

**[Appendix A --- Statutory Text: Key Provisions of S.2314]{.underline}**

The following excerpts reproduce or closely paraphrase the most relevant provisions of the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (S.2314) for ease of reference. Annotations identifying the specific ClinIQ™ modules and Meridian facilities implicated by each provision are provided in brackets.

**Section 2. Definitions.**

**(a) \"Artificial intelligence clinical decision-support tool\" or \"covered AI system\"** means any system employing machine learning, deep learning, natural language processing, or other algorithmic techniques to assist, inform, or generate clinical recommendations, diagnoses, treatment pathways, or other outputs that are used, or reasonably expected to be used, in the clinical care of patients at a covered facility.

> *Annotation: Each of the three ClinIQ™ modules --- Sepsis Early Warning, Radiology Diagnostic Support, and Medication Interaction Analysis --- constitutes a covered AI system under this definition. Each module employs machine learning or deep learning techniques and generates outputs used in clinical care at Meridian\'s NJ facilities.*

**(b) \"Covered entity\"** means any healthcare facility, healthcare system, or provider organization that is licensed or operating within the State of New Jersey and that deploys, utilizes, or integrates a covered AI system in clinical decision-making processes.

> *Annotation: Meridian Health Systems, Inc. is a covered entity by virtue of its operation of three licensed hospitals in New Jersey: Meridian Atlantic Medical Center (Atlantic City), Meridian South Jersey Regional Hospital (Egg Harbor Township), and Meridian Princeton Medical Center (Plainsboro).*

**(c) \"Independent auditor\"** means an individual or entity that (i) has no financial interest, direct or indirect, in the vendor that developed, licensed, or provides the covered AI system; and (ii) has had no role in the development, marketing, or deployment of the covered AI system under review.

> *Annotation: Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC meets the independence criteria. Langford has no financial interest in Cognitive Foundry, Inc. and has had no role in the development or deployment of the ClinIQ™ Platform.*

**Section 4. Algorithmic Transparency.**

**(a)** A covered entity shall ensure that algorithmic transparency documentation is created and maintained for each covered AI system deployed at its facilities within the State of New Jersey. Such documentation shall include:

> \(1\) Documentation of model training data sources, including the provenance, demographic composition, and representativeness of the training data;
>
> \(2\) Documentation of the validation methodology employed, including identification of internal and external validation datasets, performance metrics, and statistical methods;
>
> \(3\) Documentation of the known limitations of the covered AI system, including identified failure modes, edge cases, and population subgroups for which performance may be degraded; and
>
> \(4\) Documentation of bias testing results, including analyses of differential performance across race, ethnicity, sex, age, socioeconomic status, language, and disability status.

**(b)** Transparency documentation shall be maintained in a form accessible to clinical leadership, the Department of Health, and --- in summarized form, written in plain language --- patients.

**(c)** Transparency documentation shall be updated to reflect material changes to the covered AI system, including model updates, retraining, changes to training data, and newly identified limitations.

> *Annotation: Meridian\'s ability to comply with this section depends on Cognitive Foundry\'s provision of the specified documentation for all three ClinIQ™ modules. The current Agreement draft does not provide Meridian with a non-discretionary right to obtain this documentation.*

**Section 5. Mandatory Independent Annual Audit.**

**(a)** A covered entity shall conduct or commission an independent annual audit of each covered AI system deployed at its facilities within the State of New Jersey.

**(b)** The audit shall, at a minimum, include: (1) review of model architecture documentation and training data provenance; (2) assessment of validation results and ongoing performance metrics; (3) evaluation of bias and fairness across demographic groups; (4) review of incident reports and adverse-event correlation data; and (5) assessment of data governance practices, including PHI handling.

**(c)** Audit reports shall be completed within ninety (90) days of the close of the audit period and shall be made available to the Department of Health upon request. The covered entity shall produce audit reports within fifteen (15) business days of a Department request.

**(d)** The first audit shall be completed within twelve (12) months of the covered AI system\'s initial deployment in clinical use at a covered facility. Subsequent audits shall be conducted annually thereafter.

> *Annotation: Assuming Target Go-Live of January 6, 2026, the first audit of ClinIQ™ must be completed by January 6, 2027. Audit fieldwork should commence no later than Q4 2026. The Agreement must provide Meridian with audit access rights sufficient for Langford to complete the statutory audit scope.*

**Section 6. Patient Notification.**

**(a)** A covered entity shall inform patients when a covered AI system is used in their clinical care decisions. Notification shall be clear, in plain language, and provided prior to or concurrent with the clinical encounter in which the covered AI system is applied.

**(b)** Notification shall include: (1) identification that a covered AI system is being used; (2) a general description of the covered AI system\'s function; (3) a statement that the AI output is reviewed by a licensed clinician; and (4) information on how to request additional information about the covered AI system.

**(c)** Notification may be provided through intake forms, electronic health record patient portals, posted notices, or direct clinician communication.

> *Annotation: Patient notification processes must be operational at all three Meridian NJ facilities before the January 6, 2026 Go-Live. Notification content should address each ClinIQ™ module deployed at the facility.*

**Section 7. Vendor Contractual Requirements.**

**(a)** A covered entity shall ensure that its contract with any vendor that provides a covered AI system includes:

> \(1\) A right of the covered entity to access sufficient model documentation to fulfill its transparency obligations under this Act;
>
> \(2\) A right of the covered entity to conduct or commission independent audits and to access model documentation necessary for completion of the audit scope specified in Section 5;
>
> \(3\) Data governance terms specifying the vendor\'s obligations regarding the handling, storage, processing, and return or destruction of PHI; and
>
> \(4\) Provisions addressing the vendor\'s obligation to cooperate with the Department of Health in connection with any inquiry or investigation related to the covered AI system.

**(b)** A contract between a covered entity and a vendor shall not waive, limit, or condition the covered entity\'s rights under this Act in a manner that would prevent the covered entity from satisfying its obligations under this Act.

> *Annotation: The Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Master Services Agreement must include each of these provisions. The anti-waiver provision in subsection (b) prohibits contractual terms that effectively restrict Meridian\'s documentation access or audit rights below the level required for compliance. Current Agreement draft does not satisfy these requirements.*

**Section 9. Enforcement.**

**(a)** The Department of Health shall enforce this Act.

**(b)** The Department may impose civil penalties of up to fifty thousand dollars (\$50,000) per violation. Each day of a continuing violation shall constitute a separate violation.

**(c)** The Department may require the suspension of a non-compliant covered AI system pending remediation.

**(d)** The Department may require production of audit reports, transparency documentation, and vendor contracts, and may conduct site inspections and interviews of clinical and administrative personnel.

**(e)** This Act does not create a private right of action. This Act does not preempt or limit existing state or federal causes of action.

> *Annotation: Penalties are assessed against Meridian as the covered entity, not against Cognitive Foundry. Indemnification provisions in the Agreement must address allocation of penalty exposure attributable to Cognitive Foundry\'s non-compliance with its contractual obligations.*

**[Appendix B --- Cross-Reference: Act Requirements to Proposed Agreement Sections]{.underline}**

The following table maps each requirement of the Act to the corresponding section(s) of the proposed Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Master Services Agreement (initial draft dated June 6, 2025), identifies gaps and deficiencies in the current draft, and provides recommended revisions. This table should be read in conjunction with the Contested Terms Matrix finalized July 1, 2025.

**Cross-Reference to Contested Terms Matrix (July 1, 2025):** The following items on the Contested Terms Matrix directly implicate Act compliance and should be resolved in a manner consistent with the recommendations in this Brief:

> • **CTM Item 2:** Audit Rights --- Scope and Frequency (Meridian Position: annual independent audit at Langford Tier 2; Cognitive Foundry Position: annual SOC 2 only, with model-level audit upon mutual agreement)
>
> • **CTM Item 3:** Model Documentation Access (Meridian Position: non-discretionary access to transparency documentation per Act § 4; Cognitive Foundry Position: discretionary access, subject to Vendor consent and NDA)
>
> • **CTM Item 7:** Indemnification --- Regulatory Penalties (Meridian Position: Vendor indemnification for regulatory penalties attributable to Vendor non-compliance, carved out from cap; Cognitive Foundry Position: regulatory penalties included within general liability cap)
>
> • **CTM Item 9:** De-Identified Data Use (Meridian Position: consent conditioned on documentation and transparency requirements; Cognitive Foundry Position: broad license for aggregated/de-identified data use)

**[Appendix C --- Langford AI Assurance Group --- Audit Tier Summary]{.underline}**

**Overview.** Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC (\"Langford\") is an independent AI assurance and audit firm specializing in the evaluation of artificial intelligence systems deployed in regulated industries, including healthcare, financial services, and pharmaceuticals. Langford has been pre-approved by both Meridian Health Systems, Inc. and Cognitive Foundry, Inc. as an acceptable independent auditor for purposes of annual compliance audits of the ClinIQ™ Platform.

Langford offers a tiered audit access framework designed to balance the regulatory compliance needs of healthcare entity clients with the intellectual property protection concerns of AI vendors. The tier structure is summarized below.

**Langford Tier 2 Access --- Annual Regulatory Compliance Audit**

Langford Tier 2 access provides the auditor with access to the following categories of information and systems:

> • **Model Architecture Documentation:** Detailed technical documentation describing the architecture of each AI model, including layer structures, activation functions, feature engineering approaches, and input/output specifications. This documentation enables the auditor to assess the model\'s design and identify potential structural sources of bias or performance limitations.
>
> • **Training Data Provenance:** Documentation describing the sources, composition, and characteristics of the data used to train and fine-tune each AI model, including institutional sources, geographic origin, temporal range, demographic composition, and any data preprocessing or augmentation techniques applied.
>
> • **Validation Results:** Complete validation reports, including internal and external validation datasets, performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, AUROC, calibration metrics, and others as applicable), and statistical analyses supporting the model\'s performance claims.
>
> • **Test Queries:** The ability to submit test queries to the AI system in a controlled, sandboxed environment to evaluate the system\'s responses to specified inputs. Test queries enable the auditor to verify that the system behaves consistently with its documented performance characteristics and to probe for edge cases or anomalous outputs.

Langford Tier 2 access is identified as the baseline level of access necessary for Langford to complete an annual regulatory compliance audit satisfying the requirements of the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (S.2314), Section 5. Tier 2 access does not require disclosure of raw model weights, proprietary source code, or other trade-secret-level intellectual property.

**Langford Tier 3 Access --- Cause-Based and Regulatory-Compelled Audits**

Langford Tier 3 access provides the auditor with access to model weights and embeddings, reviewed within a secure computational enclave maintained and controlled by Langford. The Tier 3 protocol is specifically designed to address situations in which a deeper level of technical review is required --- for example, where:

> • The NJ Department of Health has initiated an investigation or inquiry that requires examination of the model\'s internal parameters;
>
> • A material adverse event or pattern of adverse events has occurred that may be attributable to the AI system\'s outputs;
>
> • The Tier 2 audit has identified anomalies or concerns that cannot be resolved without examination of model weights or internal representations; or
>
> • Other circumstances exist that reasonably require a deeper level of technical review than Tier 2 provides.

**Secure Enclave Protocol.** Under the Tier 3 protocol, model weights and embeddings are loaded into a secure computational enclave operated by Langford. The enclave is a controlled computing environment with the following characteristics:

> • No data extraction is permitted from the enclave. Auditors may view and analyze model weights and embeddings within the enclave but may not copy, download, transmit, or otherwise extract any data from the enclave environment.
>
> • All enclave access is logged, monitored, and auditable by both Langford and the AI vendor.
>
> • The enclave environment is isolated from Langford\'s general network and from the internet; no external connectivity is permitted during enclave sessions.
>
> • Upon completion of the Tier 3 audit, all data in the enclave is cryptographically destroyed, and destruction is certified in writing to both the covered entity and the vendor.

**Reconciliation with Vendor CTO Position.** The Vendor CTO\'s stated position --- that access to raw model weights is a \"hard no\" --- is specifically directed at extractable access to model weights. The Langford Tier 3 secure enclave protocol does not provide extractable access. The auditor reviews model weights within a controlled environment from which no data can be removed. This distinction is critical: Tier 3 enclave access permits the level of technical review that may be required by NJ DOH enforcement actions without creating the model-replication or reverse-engineering risk that motivates Cognitive Foundry\'s intellectual property concerns. Whitfield Ames believes that the Tier 3 enclave protocol, coupled with Langford\'s confidentiality protections, provides a reasonable basis for resolving the current impasse on this issue.

**Langford Confidentiality Protections**

Langford\'s engagement protocols include the following confidentiality protections, each of which should be memorialized in the Meridian--Cognitive Foundry Agreement or in a separate tripartite confidentiality agreement:

> • **Non-Disclosure Agreement with Vendor:** Langford executes a separate NDA directly with the AI vendor (Cognitive Foundry) prior to commencing any audit, covering all vendor-proprietary information accessed during the audit process. The NDA includes standard trade-secret protections, non-compete provisions applicable to the audit team, and liquidated damages for breach.
>
> • **Secure-Environment-Only Access:** All access to vendor-proprietary information occurs within Langford\'s secure audit environment. No vendor-proprietary data is stored on Langford\'s general systems, personal devices, or cloud storage.
>
> • **No Data Extraction:** No raw data, model weights, source code, or other vendor-proprietary information may be extracted from the secure audit environment. Audit findings are documented in narrative audit reports that describe the auditor\'s conclusions without reproducing proprietary technical details.
>
> • **Mandatory Work Product Destruction:** Upon delivery of the final audit report to Meridian, all work product, notes, interim analyses, and residual data from the audit are destroyed. Destruction is certified in writing to both Meridian and Cognitive Foundry.

**Credentials.**

> **Managing Director:** Dr. Anil Cherian, Ph.D. (Computer Science, MIT), M.S. (Biomedical Informatics, Stanford). Dr. Cherian has over fifteen years of experience in AI system evaluation and assurance, with particular expertise in healthcare AI, algorithmic fairness, and regulatory compliance. Dr. Cherian has led over forty independent audits of clinical AI systems for health systems, pharmaceutical companies, and medical device manufacturers, and has served as an expert consultant to the FDA\'s Center for Devices and Radiological Health on AI/ML-based software as a medical device.
>
> **Firm:** Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC 101 Federal Street, Suite 1900 Boston, MA 02110

This memorandum is intended solely to provide legal analysis and recommendations to Meridian Health Systems, Inc. in connection with the matters described herein. It does not constitute legal advice to any other person or entity and should not be relied upon by any person or entity other than the addressees. The analysis herein is based on the text of S.2314 as enacted and effective March 1, 2025, and on the facts and circumstances known to Whitfield Ames LLP as of the date of this memorandum. Changes in law, regulation, or facts may affect the analysis and recommendations contained herein. Whitfield Ames LLP undertakes no obligation to update this memorandum.
