> **[ARTICLE 11 --- CONFIDENTIALITY]{.underline}**
>
> **11.1** **Obligations.** Each Party (as the Receiving Party) shall: (a) hold the Disclosing Party\'s Confidential Information in strict confidence using at least the same degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than a reasonable degree of care; (b) not disclose the Disclosing Party\'s Confidential Information to any third party, except to the Receiving Party\'s employees, Affiliates, contractors, advisors, and agents who have a need to know such information for the purposes of this Agreement and who are bound by confidentiality obligations at least as protective as those set forth herein; (c) not use the Disclosing Party\'s Confidential Information for any purpose other than the performance of the Receiving Party\'s obligations or the exercise of its rights under this Agreement; and (d) promptly notify the Disclosing Party of any actual or suspected unauthorized disclosure or use of the Disclosing Party\'s Confidential Information.
>
> **11.2** **Exceptions.** The obligations set forth in Section 11.1 shall not apply to information that the Receiving Party can demonstrate: (a) was publicly available at the time of disclosure, or subsequently became publicly available through no fault or breach by the Receiving Party; (b) was independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to or use of the Disclosing Party\'s Confidential Information, as evidenced by the Receiving Party\'s contemporaneous written records; (c) was rightfully received by the Receiving Party from a third party without restriction on disclosure and without breach of any obligation of confidentiality; (d) was already known to the Receiving Party at the time of disclosure, as evidenced by the Receiving Party\'s contemporaneous written records; or (e) is required to be disclosed by applicable law, regulation, court order, or governmental authority, provided that the Receiving Party: (i) provides the Disclosing Party with prompt written notice of such requirement (to the extent legally permitted); (ii) cooperates with the Disclosing Party, at the Disclosing Party\'s expense, in seeking a protective order or other appropriate remedy to limit the scope and manner of disclosure; and (iii) discloses only the minimum amount of Confidential Information required to comply with such requirement.
>
> **11.3** **Injunctive Relief.** Each Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the other Party\'s Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm for which monetary damages would be an inadequate remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive or other equitable relief in any court of competent jurisdiction, without the necessity of proving actual damages or posting any bond or other security, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
>
> **11.4** **Survival.** The confidentiality obligations set forth in this Article 11 shall survive the termination or expiration of this Agreement for a period of five (5) years from the date of termination or expiration; provided, however, that obligations with respect to information that constitutes a trade secret under applicable law (including, without limitation, the Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S.C. § 1836 et seq., and applicable state trade secret laws) shall continue for so long as such information remains a trade secret under applicable law, without time limitation.
>

> \(b\) **General Defense Control.** Subject to subsection (c), the Indemnifying Party shall have primary control of the defense and settlement of indemnified claims, provided no settlement shall impose liability or admit fault on behalf of the Indemnified Party without its prior written consent (not to be unreasonably withheld); and
>
> \(c\) **Customer Defense Control --- Patient-Safety Matters.** Notwithstanding subsection (b), with respect to any Patient-Harm Claim or any claim involving Patient-Safety Liability: (i) Customer shall have the right to assume and control the defense of such claim, including selection of counsel; (ii) Vendor shall cooperate fully in such defense at Vendor\'s expense; (iii) Vendor shall not settle any such claim without Customer\'s prior written consent; and (iv) Vendor\'s indemnification obligation (including duty to pay damages, costs, and attorneys\' fees) shall apply regardless of whether Customer controls the defense; and

> **[ARTICLE 14 --- TERMINATION]{.underline}**

The notice of termination for cause shall specify in reasonable detail the nature of the breach. In the case of a material breach that is not reasonably capable of cure (as determined by the non-breaching Party in its reasonable judgment), the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon delivery of written notice to the breaching Party specifying the nature of the incurable breach.
>
> **14.2** **Termination for Insolvency.** Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the other Party if the other Party:
>
> \(a\) files a voluntary petition in bankruptcy or is adjudicated bankrupt or insolvent under any applicable bankruptcy, insolvency, or similar law;
>
> \(b\) makes a general assignment for the benefit of creditors;
>
> \(c\) has a receiver, trustee, custodian, or similar official appointed for all or substantially all of its assets, and such appointment is not dismissed or vacated within sixty (60) days;
>
> \(d\) commences or has commenced against it any proceeding under any bankruptcy, reorganization, insolvency, dissolution, or liquidation law, and such proceeding is not dismissed within sixty (60) days; or
>
> \(e\) ceases to conduct business in the ordinary course or is dissolved or wound up (other than in connection with a merger, consolidation, or reorganization in which the surviving entity assumes all obligations under this Agreement).

> **[EXHIBIT C --- STATEMENT OF WORK / IMPLEMENTATION PLAN]{.underline}**
>
> **1. Scope of Implementation Services**
>
> Vendor shall provide the following implementation services in connection with the deployment of the ClinIQ™ Platform (version 4.2) across Customer\'s fourteen (14) Covered Facilities:
>
> \(a\) **Project Governance and Personnel.** Vendor shall designate a dedicated project manager and a qualified implementation team consisting of clinical informaticists, integration engineers, data migration specialists, and AI/ML engineers. Customer shall designate a project lead and shall identify key stakeholders from clinical, IT, compliance, and administrative departments. The Parties shall establish a joint project steering committee that meets bi-weekly (or more frequently as mutually agreed) to review progress, address issues, and make decisions regarding project scope, timeline, and risk management.
>
> \(b\) **Environment Provisioning.** Vendor shall provision and configure the Hosting Environment (AWS GovCloud us-east-1) in accordance with the security and technical requirements set forth in Exhibit E, including network architecture, access controls, encryption configurations, and disaster recovery infrastructure.
>
> \(c\) **Data Migration.** Vendor shall develop and execute a data migration plan for the ingestion of historical clinical data from Customer\'s existing systems into the Platform, including data extraction, transformation, loading (ETL), validation, and reconciliation procedures.
>
> \(d\) **Integration Development.** Vendor shall develop, test, and deploy the integration interfaces described in Section 3.2, including: (i) HL7 FHIR R4 and HL7 v2 interfaces with Epic EHR \"MeridianConnect\"; (ii) DICOM interface with Philips PACS; and (iii) integration with BD Pyxis medication management systems. Integration specifications, including message formats, data mappings, and error-handling procedures, shall be documented and mutually agreed upon by the Parties prior to development.
>
> \(e\) **Model Configuration and Fine-Tuning.** Vendor shall configure and, as appropriate, fine-tune the Platform\'s AI/ML models using Customer Data and Customer-specific clinical protocols, formularies, and workflow parameters, in accordance with Vendor\'s standard model configuration methodology. Customer shall provide Vendor with access to representative clinical data sets and clinical subject matter experts to support model configuration and validation activities.
>
> \(f\) **Validation Testing.** Vendor shall conduct internal validation testing of the configured and integrated Platform prior to delivery for user acceptance testing, including functional testing, integration testing, performance testing, and clinical validation of model outputs against agreed-upon benchmarks.
>
> \(g\) **User Acceptance Testing.** Vendor shall support Customer in conducting user acceptance testing in accordance with the acceptance criteria and procedures set forth in Section 3 of this Exhibit C.
>
> **2. Target Go-Live Date**
>
> The target Go-Live Date is **January 6, 2026**. The Parties shall use commercially reasonable efforts to adhere to the following phased deployment schedule:

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Phase**               **Description**                                                       **Target Completion Date**
  ----------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------
  Phase 1                 Project Kickoff, Governance Establishment, Environment Provisioning   September 30, 2025

  Phase 2                 Data Migration, Integration Development (Epic, PACS, Pyxis)           November 15, 2025

  Phase 3                 Model Configuration and Customer-Specific Fine-Tuning                 November 30, 2025

  Phase 4                 Internal Validation Testing                                           December 7, 2025

  Phase 5                 User Acceptance Testing                                               December 8 -- December 31, 2025

  Phase 6                 Go-Live (Production Deployment Across All 14 Covered Facilities)      January 6, 2026
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **3. User Acceptance Testing Criteria and Procedures**
>
> Customer shall conduct user acceptance testing based on the following acceptance criteria, which the Parties shall mutually agree upon and document in a UAT plan prior to the commencement of Phase 5:
>
> \(a\) All integration interfaces (Epic, PACS, Pyxis) successfully transmit and receive data in accordance with agreed-upon specifications with an error rate of less than 0.1%;
>
> \(b\) All clinical decision-support modules (sepsis prediction, radiology image analysis, medication interaction checking) generate Outputs in accordance with the Documentation;
>
> \(c\) Platform response times for clinical alert generation are within acceptable thresholds as defined in the UAT plan;
>
> \(d\) All user authentication, authorization, and access control mechanisms function as specified in Exhibit E;
>
> \(e\) No Severity 1 or Severity 2 defects remain unresolved at the time of acceptance.
>
> **4. Training Plan**
>
> Vendor shall provide the following training during the implementation period:
>
> \(a\) **On-Site Training.** Vendor shall conduct a minimum of one hundred sixty (160) hours of instructor-led training sessions at designated Covered Facilities for clinical staff (physicians, nurses, pharmacists), IT support staff, and administrative users. Training sessions shall be scheduled in coordination with Customer to minimize disruption to clinical operations.
>
> \(b\) **Virtual Training.** Vendor shall provide access to an online learning management system containing self-paced training modules, video tutorials, and interactive guides for all Authorized Users.
>
> \(c\) **Train-the-Trainer.** Vendor shall conduct dedicated train-the-trainer sessions for Customer-designated super-users at each Covered Facility, who shall serve as internal resources for ongoing training and support.
>
> \(d\) **Training Materials.** Vendor shall provide comprehensive training materials in English, including user guides, quick-reference cards, FAQs, and video recordings of training sessions.
>
> **5. Project Milestones and Payment Schedule**
>
> Project milestones are aligned with the Implementation Fee payment schedule set forth in Exhibit B. Vendor shall provide Customer with written notice upon achievement of each milestone, together with supporting documentation as reasonably requested by Customer.
>
> **[EXHIBIT D --- SERVICE LEVEL AGREEMENT]{.underline}**
>
> **1. Platform Availability**
>
> Vendor shall maintain Platform Availability of not less than **99.9%** per calendar month, excluding Scheduled Maintenance Windows. Platform Availability shall be calculated as follows:
>
> **Platform Availability (%) = \[(Total Minutes in Calendar Month − Unscheduled Downtime Minutes) ÷ Total Minutes in Calendar Month\] × 100**
>
> Unscheduled Downtime shall be measured from the time the Platform becomes materially unavailable (as determined by Vendor\'s automated monitoring systems or upon confirmed report from Customer) until the Platform is restored to full operational status.
>
> **2. Scheduled Maintenance Windows**
>
> Vendor shall perform scheduled maintenance during the following pre-designated Scheduled Maintenance Windows:
>
> • **Day:** Sundays
>
> • **Time:** 2:00 AM -- 6:00 AM Eastern Time
>
> Vendor shall provide Customer with at least **seventy-two (72) hours\'** advance written notice of any scheduled maintenance activity, including a description of the maintenance to be performed and the anticipated duration of downtime (if any). Vendor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to minimize downtime during Scheduled Maintenance Windows and to perform maintenance activities in a manner that does not materially disrupt clinical operations.
>
> **3. Incident Severity Classifications**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Severity Level**          **Definition**                                                                                                                                                                                **Acknowledgment Target**   **Resolution Target**
  --------------------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- --------------------------- -----------------------
  **Severity 1 (Critical)**   Platform is completely unavailable across all Covered Facilities, or a critical clinical function (e.g., sepsis alerting) is non-operational, presenting a potential risk to patient safety   15 minutes                  4 hours

  **Severity 2 (High)**       A major feature or function is impaired or a significant portion of Authorized Users cannot access the Platform, significantly impacting clinical workflows with no available workaround      1 hour                      8 hours

  **Severity 3 (Medium)**     A feature or function is impaired but a reasonable workaround is available; limited or moderate impact on clinical workflows                                                                  4 hours                     2 business days

  **Severity 4 (Low)**        Minor issue, cosmetic defect, documentation error, or general inquiry with no material impact on clinical operations                                                                          1 business day              5 business days
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Resolution targets represent Vendor\'s commercially reasonable target timeframes and are not guarantees. Vendor shall provide Customer with regular status updates during the resolution of Severity 1 and Severity 2 incidents (at least hourly for Severity 1 and every two (2) hours for Severity 2).
>
> **4. Service Level Credits**
>
> In the event Vendor fails to achieve the Platform Availability target in any calendar month, Customer shall be entitled to service level credits calculated as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Monthly Platform Availability**   **Service Level Credit**
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  99.0% to less than 99.9%            5% of Monthly Fee (\$35,625)

  98.0% to less than 99.0%            10% of Monthly Fee (\$71,250)

  Below 98.0%                         15% of Monthly Fee (\$106,875)
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

> The Monthly Fee for purposes of credit calculation is \$712,500 (1/12 of \$8,550,000). Service level credits shall not exceed **fifteen percent (15%)** of the applicable Monthly Fee in any single calendar month. Credits shall be applied against the next quarterly invoice. Credits are not payable in cash, are non-transferable, and may not be carried forward beyond the current Contract Year.
>
> **5. Credit Request Procedures**
>
> To receive a service level credit, Customer must submit a written credit request to Vendor\'s support team within **thirty (30) calendar days** of the end of the calendar month in which the SLA failure occurred. The credit request shall include: (a) the date(s) and time(s) of the SLA failure( s); (b) the duration of each period of Unscheduled Downtime; and (c) any available documentation or system logs supporting the claim. Vendor shall review each credit request and respond within fifteen (15) business days. In the event of a dispute regarding a credit request, the Parties shall escalate the matter to their respective designated executives for resolution.
>
> **6. Sole and Exclusive Remedy**
>
> The service level credits set forth in this Exhibit D are Customer\'s sole and exclusive remedy, and Vendor\'s sole and exclusive liability, for any failure to meet the Platform Availability SLA. Notwithstanding the foregoing, service level credits do not limit Customer\'s other remedies under the Agreement, including termination rights under Article 14 and indemnification under Article 13.
>
> **7. Business Continuity and Disaster Recovery**
>
> Vendor shall maintain business continuity and disaster recovery capabilities for the Hosting Environment with the following objectives:
>
> • **Recovery Point Objective (RPO):** 1 hour
>
> • **Recovery Time Objective (RTO):** 4 hours
>
> Vendor shall test its disaster recovery plan at least once per year and shall provide Customer with a written summary of test results within thirty (30) days of each test.
>
> **8. AI Model Performance \[DELETED: Metrics (Informational)\] \[ADDED: --- Binding Performance Benchmarks\]**
>
> \[ADDED: Vendor warrants that the Platform\'s clinical decision-support modules shall meet or exceed the following minimum Performance Benchmarks, measured on a quarterly basis using validation methodologies agreed upon by the Parties:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Clinical Module**               **Sensitivity (Minimum)**   **Specificity (Minimum)**
  --------------------------------- --------------------------- ---------------------------
  Sepsis Prediction                 ≥ 92%                       ≥ 88%

  Radiology Image Analysis          ≥ 90%                       ≥ 85%

  Medication Interaction Checking   ≥ 95%                       ≥ 90%
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Vendor shall conduct quarterly validation testing and deliver written validation reports within thirty (30) days of the end of each calendar quarter. Escalating performance-based service credits (5%/10%/15% of quarterly fees) shall apply in accordance with Section 9.3(d) of the Agreement. If any clinical module fails to meet its Performance Benchmark for two (2) consecutive calendar quarters, Customer shall have the right to terminate the Agreement upon sixty (60) days\' written notice without payment of any early termination fee, as set forth in Section 9.3(e) and Section 14.7 of the Agreement.
>
> Bias and fairness testing metrics (demographic parity, equalized odds, and disparate-impact analysis across patient subpopulations) shall be reported quarterly as informational SLA components in the Transparency Report described in Section 10.1 of the Agreement.\]
>
> **[EXHIBIT E --- SECURITY AND TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS]{.underline}**
>
> **1. Encryption Standards**
>
> \(a\) **Data at Rest:** All Customer Data stored within the Hosting Environment, including database contents, file systems, backups, and archival media, shall be encrypted using AES-256 or equivalent encryption standards as recognized by NIST.
>
> \(b\) **Data in Transit:** All data transmitted between Customer\'s systems and the Hosting Environment, and between components within the Hosting Environment, shall be encrypted using TLS 1.2 or higher. Deprecated cipher suites and protocols (including SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1) shall be disabled.
>
> **2. Customer-Managed Encryption Key Escrow**
>
> Customer shall have the right to manage its own encryption keys through AWS Key Management Service (KMS), utilizing Customer-controlled customer master keys (CMKs). The key management arrangement shall include:
>
> \(a\) Customer shall create and control CMKs within its own AWS KMS account;
>
> \(b\) Vendor shall configure the Hosting Environment to use Customer\'s CMKs for encryption and decryption of Customer Data;
>
> \(c\) Key rotation shall be performed at least annually, or more frequently as required by Customer\'s security policies;
>
> \(d\) Vendor shall not have the ability to access Customer Data without use of the Customer-controlled CMKs;
>
> \(e\) Upon termination or expiration of the Agreement, Customer may revoke Vendor\'s access to CMKs following completion of data return or destruction obligations under Section 14.6.
>
> **3. Hosting Environment**
>
> The Platform shall be hosted in **AWS GovCloud region us-east-1**, which meets the requirements for ITAR, FedRAMP High, and other U.S. government compliance standards. Vendor shall maintain the Hosting Environment in accordance with AWS\'s shared responsibility model, with Vendor responsible for all customer-layer security obligations.
>
> **4. Access Controls and Identity Management**
>
> \(a\) All Authorized Users shall be authenticated through Customer\'s identity provider (IdP) via SAML 2.0 or OpenID Connect federation;
>
> \(b\) Multi-factor authentication (MFA) shall be required for all Authorized Users accessing the Platform;
>
> \(c\) Role-based access control (RBAC) shall be implemented to restrict user access to the minimum necessary data and functionality;
>
> \(d\) Vendor administrative access to the Hosting Environment and Customer Data shall be limited to authorized Vendor personnel on a need-to-know basis, with all administrative access logged and auditable;
>
> \(e\) Access credentials shall be unique to each individual; shared or generic accounts are prohibited.
>
> **5. Vulnerability Management and Penetration Testing**
>
> \(a\) Vendor shall conduct automated vulnerability scans of the Platform and Hosting Environment at least monthly;
>
> \(b\) Vendor shall engage a qualified third-party firm to conduct penetration testing of the Platform at least annually and shall remediate identified critical and high-severity vulnerabilities within thirty (30) days of identification;
>
> \(c\) Vendor shall apply security patches and updates to all components of the Hosting Environment in accordance with the following cadence: critical patches within seventy-two (72) hours; high-severity patches within seven (7) days; medium-severity patches within thirty (30) days;
>
> \(d\) Vendor shall provide Customer with annual summaries of vulnerability scan and penetration test results upon request.
>
> **6. HIPAA / HITECH Technical Safeguards and NIST Alignment**
>
> Vendor\'s information security program shall be aligned with the NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) and shall address all applicable HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguard requirements, including:
>
> \(a\) Access controls (45 C.F.R. § 164.312(a));
>
> \(b\) Audit controls (45 C.F.R. § 164.312(b));
>
> \(c\) Integrity controls (45 C.F.R. § 164.312(c));
>
> \(d\) Person or entity authentication (45 C.F.R. § 164.312(d));
>
> \(e\) Transmission security (45 C.F.R. § 164.312(e)).
>
> **7. Logging, Monitoring, and Audit Trails**
>
> \(a\) Vendor shall maintain comprehensive audit logs of all access to Customer Data, including user identity, date and time, actions performed, and data accessed;
>
> \(b\) Audit logs shall be retained for a minimum of seven (7) years or such longer period as required by applicable law;
>
> \(c\) Vendor shall implement real-time security monitoring and alerting for anomalous access patterns, unauthorized access attempts, and potential security incidents;
>
> \(d\) Audit logs shall be made available to Customer upon reasonable request for compliance and audit purposes.
>
> **8. Data Residency**
>
> All Customer Data, including backups, replicas, and disaster recovery copies, shall reside within the continental United States at all times, as set forth in Section 4.2 of the Agreement.
>
> **9. Subprocessor Management**
>
> Vendor shall comply with the subprocessor management and notification requirements set forth in Section 4.5 of the Agreement, including maintaining written data processing agreements with all subprocessors and ensuring subprocessor compliance with the security requirements of this Exhibit E.
>
> **[EXHIBIT F --- BUSINESS ASSOCIATE AGREEMENT]{.underline}**
>
> This Exhibit F consists of the full text of the Business Associate Agreement between Cognitive Foundry, Inc. and Meridian Health Systems, Inc., executed as of June 2, 2025, governing the Parties\' respective obligations with respect to the use, disclosure, and safeguarding of Protected Health Information under the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 (HIPAA), as amended by the Health Information Technology for Economic and Clinical Health Act (HITECH), and their implementing regulations (45 C.F.R. Parts 160 and 164), as well as applicable state data privacy and security laws, including the laws of Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey.
>
> The executed Business Associate Agreement is attached hereto in its entirety and incorporated herein by reference. In the event of any conflict between the terms of this Agreement and the terms of the BAA with respect to the use, disclosure, or safeguarding of Protected Health Information, the BAA shall control.
>
> *\[Full text of the executed BAA dated June 2, 2025, to be attached hereto.\]*
>
> **[EXHIBIT G --- VENDOR INSURANCE SUMMARY]{.underline}**
>
> **Certificate of Insurance --- Cognitive Foundry, Inc.**
>
> The following summarizes the insurance coverages maintained by Vendor as required by Article 15 of the Agreement. Certificates of insurance evidencing the coverages described below, together with applicable additional insured endorsements, are attached hereto.

NM-CY-2025-04417   \$30,000,000 (to be increased from current \$25,000,000 within ninety (90) days of Effective Date)   \$60,000,000 (to be increased from current \$50,000,000 within ninety (90) days of Effective Date)

  Workers\' Compensation                        Northfield Mutual Insurance Co.   NM-WC-2025-04418   Statutory Limits                                                                                     Statutory Limits

  Umbrella / Excess Liability                   Northfield Mutual Insurance Co.   NM-UE-2025-04419   \$10,000,000                                                                                         \$10,000,000
  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

> **Additional Insured Endorsement:** Meridian Health Systems, Inc. is named as an additional insured under the Commercial General Liability policy (NM-GL-2025-04415) and the Umbrella/Excess Liability policy (NM-UE-2025-04419).
>
> **Policy Period:** July 1, 2025 -- June 30, 2026 (subject to annual renewal).
>

>
> **Broker of Record:** Hartfield & Associates Insurance Brokers, LLC, 500 Market Street, Suite 1200, San Francisco, CA 94105.
>
> **Tail Coverage:** Vendor shall maintain tail or extended-reporting-period coverage under its Professional Liability (NM-PL-2025-04416) and Cyber Liability (NM-CY-2025-04417) policies for a period of not less than three (3) years following expiration or termination of this Agreement, as required by Section 15.4.
>
> *\[Certificates of insurance and endorsements to be attached hereto.\]*
>
> \[ADDED: \<!\-- pagebreak \--\>
>
> **[EXHIBIT H --- INDEPENDENT AUDITOR ENGAGEMENT TERMS]{.underline}**
>
> **1. Pre-Approved Independent Auditor**
>
> The Parties agree that the following firm is the pre-approved Independent Auditor for purposes of Section 10.3 of the Agreement:
>
> **Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC** 101 Federal Street, Suite 1900 Boston, MA 02110 Managing Director: Dr. Anil Cherian
>
> **2. Scope of Audit Access**
>
> The Independent Auditor shall have access to the following categories of information, organized by tier:
>
> **Tier 2 Access (Standard --- Quarterly Audits):**
>
> • Model architecture documentation, including data-flow diagrams, algorithm descriptions, and network topology
>
> • Training data provenance records, including data sources, data preparation methodologies, and de-identification procedures
>
> • Validation and test datasets and results, including performance metrics by subpopulation
>
> • Feature-importance rankings and explainability outputs (e.g., SHAP values, LIME explanations)
>
> • Bias and fairness testing results and methodologies
>

> **Tier 3 Access (Cause-Based --- Investigations, Regulatory Inquiries, NJ DOH Examinations):**

>

> **3. Confidentiality Requirements**
>

>
> • All reviews of Tier 3 materials shall be conducted solely within Vendor\'s designated secure enclave environment
>
> • No data extraction, copying, or removal of Vendor Confidential Information from the secure environment shall be permitted
>
> • All working papers containing Vendor Confidential Information shall be destroyed within thirty (30) days of delivery of the audit report to Customer
>
> • The Independent Auditor shall provide written certification of destruction to Vendor within five (5) business days of completing destruction
>
> **4. Customer Designated Recipients of Audit Reports**
>
> Audit reports shall be delivered to the following Customer representatives:
>
> • Dr. Priya Narayanan, SVP & General Counsel
>
> • Marcus Delgado, VP, Digital Health & Technology Transactions
>
> **5. Audit Report Deliverables**
>

>
> • Written findings summarizing the scope, methodology, and results of the audit
>
> • Identification of risks, deficiencies, or areas of concern
>
> • Remediation recommendations
>
> **6. Vendor Response**
>
> Vendor shall provide a written response and remediation plan within thirty (30) days of receipt of any audit report identifying material findings.
>
> **7. Substitute Auditor Process**
>
> If Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC is unable or unwilling to serve as Independent Auditor:
>
> • Customer may propose an alternative Independent Auditor
>
> • Vendor shall have reasonable approval rights (not to be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed)
>
> • The Parties shall negotiate in good faith to agree upon a qualified substitute within thirty (30) days
>
> **8. Regulatory Cooperation**
>
> • Vendor shall cooperate fully with the NJ Department of Health and other governmental authorities exercising jurisdiction under the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (effective March 1, 2025) or any comparable state or federal regulation
>
> • Vendor shall not seek protective orders or confidential treatment that would prevent Customer from satisfying its regulatory obligations, except with Customer\'s prior written consent
>
> • Vendor acknowledges that three (3) of the fourteen (14) Covered Facilities are located in New Jersey and are subject to the NJ Act, including mandatory independent annual audits with reports available to the NJ Department of Health\]
>
> *--- End of Agreement ---*

**[LANGFORD AI ASSURANCE GROUP, LLC]{.underline}**

**[Proposed Audit Scope for ClinIQ™ Platform (Preliminary)]{.underline}**

**Independent AI Model Audit Services**

 

**Prepared for:** Meridian Health Systems, Inc.

**Prepared by:** Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC

**Date:** June 25, 2025

**Document Classification:** Confidential --- Draft for Discussion

 

Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC 101 Federal Street, Suite 1900 Boston, MA 02110

 

Dr. Anil Cherian, Managing Director

Right-click to update Table of Contents

**[1. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

On behalf of Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC (\"Langford\"), I am pleased to present this preliminary proposal to Meridian Health Systems, Inc. (\"Meridian\") in connection with the opportunity to serve as independent AI model auditor for the ClinIQ™ platform (version 4.2), developed and operated by Cognitive Foundry, Inc. (\"Cognitive Foundry\"). We appreciate Meridian\'s confidence in approaching Langford to evaluate the scope and structure of an independent audit engagement, and we recognize the significance of this undertaking given the clinical importance of the ClinIQ™ platform and its anticipated role across Meridian\'s healthcare delivery network.

This preliminary proposal outlines Langford\'s proposed scope, methodology, tiered access requirements, confidentiality framework, audit cadence, and estimated costs for an independent audit of the ClinIQ™ v4.2 platform as deployed across Meridian\'s fourteen (14) covered hospital facilities in Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey. The ClinIQ™ platform, upon full deployment, is expected to serve an estimated 2.1 million annual patient encounters and will be accessible to approximately 6,200 named licensed users --- a scale of deployment that underscores the critical importance of rigorous, independent model assurance. The breadth of the deployment, spanning three states and encompassing diverse patient populations, introduces both clinical and regulatory considerations that this proposal is designed to address comprehensively.

Langford\'s healthcare AI audit framework has been purpose-built to address both clinical safety imperatives and the evolving regulatory landscape governing the use of artificial intelligence and machine-learning systems in healthcare settings. Of particular relevance to this engagement, the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act, which became effective on March 1, 2025, mandates that healthcare entities deploying covered AI clinical decision-support systems in the State of New Jersey conduct independent annual audits and make the resulting audit reports available to the New Jersey Department of Health upon request. Non-compliance with this statute carries civil penalties of up to \$50,000 per violation. Three of Meridian\'s fourteen facilities are located in New Jersey and are therefore subject to this mandate, making the establishment of a robust, independent audit program not merely advisable but legally required. Langford\'s proposed methodology has been designed to satisfy the requirements of the New Jersey statute while also addressing federal guidance and best practices applicable across all of Meridian\'s facilities.

Langford is committed to conducting rigorous, independent assessments of the ClinIQ™ platform while simultaneously respecting Cognitive Foundry\'s legitimate intellectual property interests. We recognize that the ClinIQ™ platform embodies substantial proprietary research and development investment, and we have structured our proposed audit tiers to balance audit depth and independence against the need to protect trade secrets, model weights, and other proprietary artifacts. Through the tiered access protocols described in this proposal, coupled with robust confidentiality protections including strict non-disclosure agreements and secure-environment-only access requirements, Langford aims to provide Meridian with meaningful, independent assurance without compromising the proprietary interests of Cognitive Foundry.

This proposal is preliminary in nature and is presented as a basis for discussion among Langford, Meridian, and Cognitive Foundry. The final scope, methodology, access arrangements, and cost structure will be subject to refinement following discussions among the parties, and will ultimately be governed by the audit access provisions incorporated into the definitive agreement between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry governing the ClinIQ™ platform deployment. Langford stands ready to engage in those discussions at Meridian\'s convenience and looks forward to the opportunity to contribute to the safe, effective, and compliant deployment of AI-enabled clinical decision support across Meridian\'s healthcare system.

Respectfully submitted,

Dr. Anil Cherian Managing Director Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC

**[2. Background and Context]{.underline}**

**2.1 Platform Overview**

The ClinIQ™ v4.2 platform is an AI-enabled clinical decision-support system developed and operated by Cognitive Foundry, Inc., a Delaware corporation headquartered in Menlo Park, California. The platform encompasses three principal clinical modules designed to assist licensed healthcare providers in clinical decision-making:

> **(a) Sepsis Prediction Module.** The sepsis prediction module utilizes machine-learning algorithms to analyze patient vital signs, laboratory values, clinical notes, and historical medical data to identify patients at elevated risk of developing sepsis or septic shock. The module generates risk scores and clinical alerts intended to facilitate early intervention by care teams.
>
> **(b) Radiology Diagnostic Assistance Module.** The radiology diagnostic assistance module applies computer vision and deep-learning techniques to medical imaging data, including chest X-rays, CT scans, and MRI studies, to assist radiologists and other interpreting physicians in identifying potential abnormalities, prioritizing worklists, and generating preliminary findings for physician review and confirmation.
>
> **(c) Medication Interaction Analysis Module.** The medication interaction analysis module evaluates active and proposed medication regimens against patient-specific clinical data, including diagnosis codes, laboratory values, genetic markers (where available), and co-morbidity profiles, to identify potential adverse drug interactions, contraindications, and dosing concerns.

The ClinIQ™ platform is deployed in a cloud-hosted environment within Amazon Web Services GovCloud (us-east-1 region), a FedRAMP-authorized infrastructure designed for sensitive government and healthcare workloads. The platform is integrated with Meridian\'s enterprise electronic health record system, Epic Systems (\"MeridianConnect\"), through industry-standard integration interfaces including HL7 FHIR APIs and Epic\'s proprietary interoperability framework. This integration enables ClinIQ™ to ingest real-time clinical data from MeridianConnect and deliver decision-support outputs directly within the clinician workflow.

The scope of the anticipated deployment encompasses fourteen (14) hospital facilities operated by Meridian Health Systems across three states: Delaware, Pennsylvania, and New Jersey. Of these fourteen facilities, three are located in the State of New Jersey and are specifically subject to the requirements of the New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act, as discussed in further detail in Section 2.2 below. The remaining eleven facilities, while not currently subject to analogous state-specific AI audit mandates, benefit from the application of consistent audit standards across the entire Meridian network. The platform is expected to support approximately 2.1 million annual patient encounters across all fourteen facilities and will be accessible to approximately 6,200 named licensed users, including physicians, advanced practice providers, nurses, pharmacists, and other clinical staff.

**2.2 Regulatory Landscape**

The deployment of AI-enabled clinical decision-support systems in healthcare settings operates within an evolving and increasingly complex regulatory environment. Langford\'s proposed audit methodology has been designed to address the following key regulatory considerations:

**FDA Clinical Decision-Support Guidance.** The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has issued guidance regarding clinical decision-support software, including the criteria under which such software may be exempt from regulation as a medical device under Section 3060 of the 21st Century Cures Act. Under the FDA\'s framework, clinical decision-support software may qualify for the statutory exemption if it meets four criteria: (i) it is not intended to acquire, process, or analyze a medical image, signal, or pattern from an in vitro diagnostic device or a pattern or signal from a signal acquisition system; (ii) it is intended for the purpose of displaying, analyzing, or printing medical information about a patient or other medical information; (iii) it is intended for the purpose of supporting or providing recommendations to a healthcare professional about prevention, diagnosis, or treatment of a disease or condition; and (iv) it is intended for the purpose of enabling such healthcare professional to independently review the basis for such recommendations so that the professional is not intended to rely primarily on the recommendation without independently reviewing the basis for the recommendation. The applicability of this exemption to each of the three ClinIQ™ clinical modules is a critical regulatory question. In particular, the radiology diagnostic assistance module\'s interaction with medical imaging data raises questions regarding the first criterion of the exemption that warrant careful analysis as part of the audit process. Langford\'s regulatory compliance verification component, described in Section 3.6, includes an assessment of whether each ClinIQ™ module satisfies or fails to satisfy the statutory exemption criteria.

**New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (Effective March 1, 2025).** The New Jersey AI in Healthcare Transparency Act (the \"NJ Act\") represents one of the most significant state-level regulatory developments affecting the deployment of AI systems in healthcare. The NJ Act applies to \"covered AI clinical decision-support systems\" deployed at healthcare facilities licensed by the State of New Jersey. Key requirements of the NJ Act include:

> \(i\) *Independent Annual Audit Requirement.* Healthcare entities deploying covered AI clinical decision-support systems at New Jersey-licensed facilities must cause an independent audit of each such system to be conducted not less than once per calendar year. The audit must be performed by a qualified independent auditor with no financial interest in the AI system developer or vendor.
>
> \(ii\) *Transparency Disclosures.* Healthcare entities must provide patients with clear and conspicuous disclosures regarding the use of AI clinical decision-support systems in their care, including information regarding the general nature and purpose of the system.
>
> \(iii\) *Documentation Obligations.* Healthcare entities must maintain comprehensive documentation regarding the AI systems deployed at their facilities, including documentation of audit findings, remediation actions, and ongoing monitoring activities.
>
> \(iv\) *Audit Report Availability.* Audit reports produced pursuant to the independent annual audit requirement must be made available to the New Jersey Department of Health upon request.
>
> \(v\) *Civil Penalties.* Non-compliance with the NJ Act may result in civil penalties of up to \$50,000 per violation, as assessed by the New Jersey Department of Health.

Three of Meridian\'s fourteen facilities are located in New Jersey and are therefore subject to the requirements of the NJ Act. It is essential that the audit methodology employed by Langford be designed to satisfy the NJ Act\'s independent annual audit requirement with respect to those three facilities, while also providing a consistent framework applicable across Meridian\'s entire network. Langford\'s proposed methodology addresses both federal guidance and the specific mandates of the NJ Act, ensuring that Meridian is positioned to demonstrate compliance upon request by the New Jersey Department of Health.

Additional state and federal regulatory developments, including potential future federal AI legislation, CMS conditions of participation, and state-specific privacy laws applicable in Delaware and Pennsylvania, may also bear on the audit scope. Langford monitors these developments on an ongoing basis and will incorporate any material regulatory changes into the audit methodology as they arise.

**2.3 Langford\'s Qualifications**

Langford AI Assurance Group, LLC is a healthcare-focused AI audit and assurance firm headquartered in Boston, Massachusetts. Founded in 2019, Langford specializes exclusively in the independent evaluation of artificial intelligence and machine-learning systems deployed in clinical and healthcare settings. Langford\'s practice areas include algorithmic bias assessment, clinical accuracy validation, model drift detection, explainability analysis, and regulatory compliance verification for healthcare AI systems.

Dr. Anil Cherian, Langford\'s Managing Director, brings over twenty years of experience at the intersection of healthcare, technology, and regulatory compliance. Dr. Cherian holds an M.D. from Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine and a Ph.D. in Biomedical Informatics from Stanford University. Prior to founding Langford, Dr. Cherian served as Chief Medical Informatics Officer at a major academic health system and subsequently led the healthcare AI assurance practice at a national consulting firm. Dr. Cherian is a recognized authority on healthcare AI governance and has contributed to industry standards development through participation in working groups convened by the National Academy of Medicine, the American Medical Informatics Association, and the Coalition for Health AI.

Langford maintains complete independence from both Meridian Health Systems, Inc. and Cognitive Foundry, Inc. Langford has no prior or current advisory, consulting, investment, licensing, or other commercial relationship with Cognitive Foundry. Similarly, Langford has no prior engagement history with Meridian beyond preliminary discussions related to this proposal. This independence is fundamental to the credibility and value of Langford\'s audit work and will be maintained throughout the engagement.

**[3. Audit Components and Methodology]{.underline}**

Langford\'s proposed audit of the ClinIQ™ v4.2 platform encompasses six core components, each addressing a distinct dimension of AI system assurance. The components are designed to function both independently and as an integrated assessment, providing Meridian with a comprehensive view of the ClinIQ™ platform\'s performance, fairness, accuracy, data quality, explainability, and regulatory compliance. The following sections describe each component in detail, including the specific methodologies Langford proposes to employ and the types of findings each component is designed to surface.

**3.1 Algorithmic Bias and Fairness Assessment**

Algorithmic bias in clinical decision-support systems poses significant risks to patient safety and health equity. Biased model outputs may result in differential quality of care across patient populations, potentially exacerbating existing health disparities. Langford\'s bias and fairness assessment component is designed to identify, quantify, and characterize potential sources of algorithmic bias in the ClinIQ™ platform.

**Demographic Categories Under Evaluation.** Langford will assess ClinIQ™ model outputs across the following protected and clinically significant demographic categories: race, ethnicity, age, sex, socioeconomic proxies (including zip code, insurance status, and payer type), primary language, and disability status. These categories reflect both legally protected classes under federal and state anti-discrimination laws and clinically significant dimensions along which health disparities are known to exist.

**Statistical Analysis of Differential Performance.** Langford will apply a battery of statistical methods to evaluate whether ClinIQ™ model performance --- including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and calibration --- varies materially across patient subpopulations defined by the demographic categories listed above. Metrics to be evaluated include, but are not limited to, equalized odds, demographic parity, predictive parity, and calibration across groups. Statistical significance testing and effect-size analysis will be employed to distinguish clinically meaningful disparities from random variation.

**Training Data Representativeness.** Langford will evaluate the demographic composition of the training data used to develop the ClinIQ™ models and compare that composition to the demographic profile of Meridian\'s patient population. This analysis is intended to identify potential representation gaps --- instances in which certain demographic groups are underrepresented in the training data relative to the population served --- that may contribute to differential model performance. The analysis will consider both the overall training dataset and any subsets, stratifications, or sampling strategies applied during model development.

**Source Identification.** Langford will investigate potential sources of algorithmic bias arising from feature selection decisions, data preprocessing pipelines, label construction methodologies, and model architecture choices. This investigation may include analysis of proxy variables --- features that are correlated with protected demographic categories and may introduce bias even if the protected category itself is not used as a direct model input. Langford will document identified bias sources and, where appropriate, recommend mitigation strategies for consideration by Cognitive Foundry and Meridian.

**3.2 Clinical Accuracy Validation**

The clinical utility of the ClinIQ™ platform depends fundamentally on the accuracy of its outputs. Langford\'s clinical accuracy validation component is designed to independently verify that each ClinIQ™ clinical module produces outputs that meet or exceed clinically acceptable performance thresholds.

**Gold-Standard Benchmarking.** Langford will validate ClinIQ™ module outputs against gold-standard clinical datasets. For each module, the appropriate gold standard will be defined in consultation with clinical subject-matter experts and may include physician-adjudicated case reviews, established clinical registries, and published benchmark datasets. Validation will be conducted using blinded comparison protocols in which ClinIQ™ outputs are evaluated against the gold standard without knowledge of the model\'s predictions, and vice versa.

**Module-Specific Evaluation Domains.** Each ClinIQ™ clinical module will be evaluated within its specific clinical domain:

> **(a) Sepsis Prediction Module.** Sensitivity and specificity benchmarking will be performed against confirmed sepsis cases identified through the Sepsis-3 consensus criteria. Evaluation will include assessment of the module\'s ability to generate timely alerts --- that is, alerts generated sufficiently in advance of clinical deterioration to permit meaningful clinical intervention. Receiver operating characteristic (ROC) analysis, precision-recall curves, and time-to-alert metrics will be reported.
>
> **(b) Radiology Diagnostic Assistance Module.** Sensitivity and specificity benchmarking will be performed for each imaging modality and clinical finding category supported by the module. Evaluation will be conducted against radiologist-adjudicated findings using a panel of board-certified radiologists, with inter-reader agreement analysis to establish the gold-standard reference. Metrics will include per-finding sensitivity and specificity, area under the ROC curve (AUC), and localization accuracy where applicable.
>
> **(c) Medication Interaction Analysis Module.** Sensitivity and specificity benchmarking will be performed against established drug interaction databases (e.g., First Databank, Lexicomp, Clinical Pharmacology) and pharmacist-adjudicated case reviews. Evaluation will include assessment of the module\'s ability to identify clinically significant interactions (as distinguished from theoretical or minor interactions), its false-positive rate for interaction alerts, and its performance across different drug classes, patient populations, and co-morbidity profiles.

**Validation Dataset Construction.** Langford will work with Meridian and, as necessary, with Cognitive Foundry to construct or select validation datasets appropriate for each module. Validation datasets will be designed to be representative of Meridian\'s patient population and clinical case mix. Where feasible, Langford will leverage de-identified patient data from Meridian\'s EHR system (MeridianConnect) to construct validation scenarios that reflect real-world clinical conditions at Meridian facilities. All validation dataset construction will comply with applicable privacy regulations, including HIPAA.

**False-Positive and False-Negative Analysis.** Langford will document false-positive and false-negative rates for each ClinIQ™ module and conduct clinical significance analysis for identified errors. This analysis will distinguish between errors that are clinically inconsequential (e.g., a false-positive medication interaction alert for a theoretical interaction with no clinical relevance) and errors that could result in patient harm (e.g., a false-negative sepsis prediction that fails to alert clinicians to a patient experiencing early sepsis). The clinical significance analysis will inform the overall risk assessment and any recommendations for model improvement.

**3.3 Model Drift Detection and Performance Degradation Monitoring**

Machine-learning models deployed in dynamic clinical environments are subject to performance degradation over time as the distribution of input data shifts, clinical practices evolve, and patient populations change. Model drift --- the gradual divergence between the data distribution on which a model was trained and the data distribution encountered in production --- can compromise model accuracy, calibration, and fairness without producing obvious or immediate indicators of failure. Langford\'s model drift detection component is designed to establish baseline performance profiles and implement ongoing monitoring for temporal drift.

**Baseline Performance Profiling.** At the time of the initial comprehensive audit, Langford will establish detailed baseline performance profiles for each ClinIQ™ module. These profiles will capture the full range of performance metrics described in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, calibration, and fairness metrics across demographic subgroups. The baseline profiles will serve as the reference against which future performance measurements are compared.

**Statistical Process Control Methods.** Langford will apply statistical process control (SPC) methods, including control charts, CUSUM (cumulative sum) analysis, and Page-Hinkley change-detection algorithms, to detect temporal trends in model performance metrics. These methods are designed to identify both gradual drift (slow, continuous degradation) and sudden shifts (abrupt performance changes, potentially attributable to data pipeline failures, EHR system updates, or changes in clinical practice patterns).

**Clinically Significant Degradation Triggers.** Langford will work with Meridian\'s clinical leadership to define thresholds for clinically significant performance degradation --- that is, the magnitude of performance decline that warrants clinical concern and potential remedial action. These thresholds will be module-specific and will account for the clinical context in which each module is used. For example, the sensitivity threshold for the sepsis prediction module may be set more aggressively than the sensitivity threshold for the medication interaction analysis module, reflecting the difference in clinical urgency and potential for patient harm.

**Retraining and Recalibration Recommendations.** When performance degradation is detected, Langford will provide recommendations regarding the need for model retraining or recalibration. These recommendations will identify the nature and likely cause of the degradation, the clinical risk implications, and the urgency of remedial action. Langford will not itself perform model retraining --- that function remains the responsibility of Cognitive Foundry --- but will provide independent assessment of whether retraining is indicated and, following retraining, whether the remedial action has been effective.

**3.4 Input Data Quality Assessment**

The performance of any machine-learning model is fundamentally dependent on the quality of its input data. In the ClinIQ™ deployment, input data flows from Meridian\'s Epic Systems EHR (MeridianConnect) through integration interfaces to the ClinIQ™ platform. Data quality issues at any point in this pipeline --- from initial data capture in the EHR to integration layer transmission to ClinIQ™ data preprocessing --- can compromise model performance and produce unreliable outputs. Langford\'s input data quality assessment is designed to evaluate the integrity of the data pipeline and identify data quality issues that may affect ClinIQ™ performance.

**Data Pipeline Evaluation.** Langford will map and evaluate the data pipelines feeding ClinIQ™ from MeridianConnect, including the HL7 FHIR API interfaces, any middleware or integration engines, data transformation layers, and data staging areas within the ClinIQ™ platform. The evaluation will assess the technical architecture of the data pipeline, including error handling, retry logic, data validation checks, and logging mechanisms.

**Data Quality Dimensions.** Langford will assess the quality of data ingested by ClinIQ™ across four principal dimensions:

> **(a) Completeness.** Assessment of the extent to which required data elements are present and populated in the data received by ClinIQ™. Missing data can result in degraded model performance, particularly when missingness is non-random and correlated with patient characteristics.
>
> **(b) Consistency.** Assessment of the extent to which data values are internally consistent (e.g., vital signs within physiologically plausible ranges, diagnosis codes consistent with clinical context) and consistent across data sources and time periods.
>
> **(c) Timeliness.** Assessment of the latency between data capture in MeridianConnect and data availability within ClinIQ™. For time-sensitive modules such as the sepsis prediction module, data latency can directly impact clinical utility.
>
> **(d) Accuracy.** Assessment of the extent to which data values received by ClinIQ™ accurately reflect the underlying clinical reality, as documented in the source EHR system. Accuracy assessment may include comparison of ClinIQ™ input data against source EHR records for a sample of patient encounters.

**Data Quality Issue Identification.** Langford will identify specific data quality issues that may compromise ClinIQ™ model performance, including systematic data gaps, encoding inconsistencies, integration errors, and data format mismatches. For each identified issue, Langford will assess the potential impact on model outputs and provide recommendations for remediation.

**Data Preprocessing Review.** Langford will review the data preprocessing and normalization steps applied by the ClinIQ™ platform to raw input data, including missing value imputation, feature scaling, categorical encoding, and outlier handling. This review will assess whether preprocessing steps are clinically appropriate, methodologically sound, and consistently applied across patient subpopulations.

**3.5 Explainability and Interpretability Analysis**

The ability of clinicians to understand and critically evaluate AI-generated recommendations is essential to the safe and effective use of clinical decision-support systems. Explainability --- the capacity of an AI system to provide human-interpretable explanations for its outputs --- is increasingly recognized as a clinical safety requirement and is relevant to the FDA\'s assessment of whether a clinical decision-support system enables independent clinical review. Langford\'s explainability analysis component evaluates whether the ClinIQ™ platform provides clinicians with meaningful, accurate, and clinically relevant explanations for its outputs.

**Feature Importance Analysis.** For each ClinIQ™ clinical module, Langford will conduct feature importance analysis to identify the clinical variables that most strongly influence model outputs. Feature importance will be assessed at both the global level (overall model behavior) and the local level (individual patient predictions). This analysis will identify cases in which model outputs are driven by clinically non-intuitive or potentially inappropriate features.

**SHAP Value Computation and Visualization.** Langford will compute SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values for representative patient scenarios across each ClinIQ™ module. SHAP values provide a mathematically rigorous decomposition of individual model predictions into the contributions of each input feature, enabling precise attribution of model outputs to specific clinical data points. Langford will generate SHAP value visualizations (force plots, summary plots, and dependence plots) and evaluate whether these visualizations, or comparable explanation mechanisms provided by the ClinIQ™ platform, accurately convey the basis for model recommendations.

**Decision Pathway Documentation.** Langford will trace model outputs to their contributing clinical inputs, documenting the \"decision pathway\" from raw patient data through feature engineering, model inference, and post-processing to the final clinician-facing recommendation. This documentation will be prepared in a format accessible to both technical and clinical reviewers and will serve as a reference for evaluating the transparency of the ClinIQ™ system.

**Clinician-Facing Explanation Assessment.** Langford will evaluate the explanations presented to clinicians through the ClinIQ™ user interface, assessing whether these explanations are accurate (i.e., faithfully represent the actual basis for the model\'s output), complete (i.e., capture the most important contributing factors), and clinically meaningful (i.e., expressed in terms that are relevant and interpretable to the intended clinical users). This assessment will include usability considerations, such as the clarity, prominence, and accessibility of explanations within the clinical workflow.

**Standards Alignment.** Langford will evaluate ClinIQ™\'s explainability features against emerging standards and best practices for healthcare AI explainability, including guidance from the Coalition for Health AI, the National Academy of Medicine, and applicable international standards such as ISO/IEC 22989 (Artificial Intelligence --- Concepts and Terminology) and ISO/IEC 23894 (Artificial Intelligence --- Guidance on Risk Management).

**3.6 Regulatory Compliance Verification**

Langford\'s regulatory compliance verification component is designed to ensure that the ClinIQ™ deployment at Meridian facilities complies with applicable federal and state regulatory requirements. This component produces documentation sufficient to support regulatory submissions or respond to inquiries from regulators, including the New Jersey Department of Health.

**FDA Clinical Decision-Support Software Compliance.** Langford will assess each ClinIQ™ clinical module against the FDA\'s criteria for clinical decision-support software, including the four-part test for statutory exemption from device regulation under Section 3060 of the 21st Century Cures Act. This assessment will identify modules that clearly qualify for the exemption, modules that clearly do not qualify, and modules that fall into ambiguous categories requiring further analysis or regulatory consultation. For any module that may not qualify for the exemption, Langford will identify the specific criteria not met and recommend next steps, which may include engagement with the FDA or modification of the module\'s functionality or user interface.

**NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act Compliance.** Langford will assess Meridian\'s compliance with the NJ Act\'s requirements as they apply to the three New Jersey facilities deploying ClinIQ™. This assessment will include:

> \(i\) Verification that the independent annual audit requirement is being satisfied through Langford\'s engagement;
>
> \(ii\) Assessment of transparency disclosures provided to patients at New Jersey facilities, including evaluation of the content, format, and delivery mechanisms of such disclosures;
>
> \(iii\) Review of documentation maintained by Meridian regarding the ClinIQ™ deployment, including audit findings, remediation actions, and monitoring records;
>
> \(iv\) Confirmation that audit reports are maintained in a form suitable for production to the New Jersey Department of Health upon request; and
>
> \(v\) Identification of any gaps in compliance or areas of regulatory risk.

**Documentation for Regulatory Submissions.** Langford\'s audit reports will be prepared in a format and with sufficient detail to support regulatory submissions or to respond to formal or informal inquiries from the New Jersey Department of Health, the FDA, or other regulatory authorities. Langford will maintain clear documentation of its methodology, data sources, analytical procedures, findings, and recommendations to facilitate regulatory review.

**Gap Analysis.** Langford will conduct a comprehensive gap analysis identifying any areas of non-compliance or elevated regulatory risk, including areas in which the ClinIQ™ platform\'s current functionality, documentation, or operational practices fall short of regulatory requirements or best practices. For each identified gap, Langford will provide a risk assessment and recommended remediation actions.

**[4. Audit Tier Definitions and Access Requirements]{.underline}**

Langford proposes a tiered access framework for the ClinIQ™ audit engagement, comprising three tiers of increasing access depth. The tiered structure is designed to balance the need for audit rigor and independence against Cognitive Foundry\'s legitimate interest in protecting its proprietary intellectual property. Each tier is appropriate for different use cases and provides a different level of assurance. The tier framework also enables the parties to calibrate the level of audit access to the specific circumstances and needs at any given time, avoiding unnecessary exposure of proprietary materials during routine monitoring while preserving access to deeper audit capabilities when warranted.

**4.1 Tier 1 --- Summary Audit**

**Description.** A Tier 1 Summary Audit is a minimal-access audit based primarily on vendor-provided information and system outputs. Tier 1 audits do not require direct access to Cognitive Foundry\'s proprietary model architecture, training data, or model internals. Instead, the auditor works with externally observable information --- model output logs, aggregate performance metrics, and vendor-supplied documentation --- to assess system performance, identify trends, and flag potential concerns for further investigation.

**Access Requirements.** The following materials and access are required for a Tier 1 Summary Audit:

> • Model output logs, both historical (covering the period since the last audit or since go-live) and ongoing (covering the audit period), including prediction outputs, confidence scores, and associated patient encounter identifiers (de-identified as appropriate);
>
> • Aggregate performance metrics as reported by Cognitive Foundry, including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, calibration statistics, and any performance dashboards or monitoring reports generated by the ClinIQ™ platform;
>
> • Vendor-provided documentation, including user manuals, technical specifications, model cards, published validation studies, release notes for any model or platform updates, and any other publicly available or vendor-disclosed technical materials.

**Use Cases.** Tier 1 audits are appropriate for periodic monitoring, interim health checks, and trend analysis between comprehensive audits. The semi-annual monitoring reports proposed in Section 6.2 of this proposal will be conducted at the Tier 1 level.

**Limitations.** Tier 1 access does not provide the auditor with sufficient access to independently verify model architecture, training methodology, feature engineering, or the root causes of identified performance issues. Because Tier 1 relies primarily on vendor-reported data and externally observable outputs, it introduces inherent constraints on audit independence --- the auditor is unable to independently confirm the completeness or accuracy of vendor-provided metrics. Accordingly, while Tier 1 audits provide valuable monitoring capability, they are not sufficient, standing alone, to satisfy the independent audit requirements of the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act or to provide the level of assurance that Langford recommends for a clinical decision-support system of the scope and criticality of ClinIQ™.

**4.2 Tier 2 --- Standard Audit**

**Description.** A Tier 2 Standard Audit is a comprehensive audit that provides the auditor with independent verification capability through direct access to model architecture documentation, data provenance information, and the ability to submit test queries to the ClinIQ™ platform and evaluate responses. Tier 2 access enables the auditor to independently assess the system\'s design, training, and behavior without relying solely on vendor-reported information, while stopping short of direct inspection of model weights, embeddings, or training pipeline code.

**Access Requirements.** Tier 2 access is cumulative and includes all Tier 1 access described above, plus the following:

> • Model architecture documentation, including network topology diagrams, layer configurations, activation functions, hyperparameter specifications, optimizer settings, regularization techniques, and any ensemble or multi-model configurations;
>
> • Training data provenance records, including documentation of data sources, data collection methodology, preprocessing steps, data cleaning and transformation procedures, labeling protocols (including labeler qualifications and inter-annotator agreement metrics), and demographic composition of the training dataset (with breakdowns by race, ethnicity, age, sex, and other relevant categories);
>
> • Validation and test result datasets and associated methodologies, including the datasets used by Cognitive Foundry to validate model performance during development, the metrics achieved, and the validation methodology employed;
>
> • The ability to submit test queries to the production ClinIQ™ environment or a mirrored environment maintained by Cognitive Foundry that replicates production model behavior. Test queries will consist of synthetic or de-identified patient scenarios submitted through the standard input interface, and the auditor will receive the full model output, including prediction values, confidence scores, and any explanatory data generated by the model\'s explainability features.

**Use Cases.** Tier 2 access is appropriate for the annual comprehensive audit, regulatory compliance verification, algorithmic bias and fairness assessment, clinical accuracy validation, and explainability analysis. The annual comprehensive audit proposed in Section 6.1 of this proposal will be conducted at the Tier 2 level as the baseline.

**Sufficiency Statement.** Tier 2 access is sufficient for most regulatory compliance purposes, including the independent annual audit required by the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act. Tier 2 provides the auditor with sufficient information to independently evaluate the ClinIQ™ platform\'s design, performance, fairness, and explainability characteristics without requiring direct access to the most sensitive proprietary artifacts (model weights, embeddings, and training pipeline code). Langford recommends Tier 2 as the standard audit tier for the ClinIQ™ engagement with Meridian. In Langford\'s professional judgment, Tier 2 access strikes an appropriate balance between audit rigor and intellectual property protection for routine annual audit purposes.

**4.3 Tier 3 --- Deep Audit**

**Description.** A Tier 3 Deep Audit provides the maximum level of audit access, including direct inspection of model weights, embedding layers, vector representations, and the training pipeline. Tier 3 is the most invasive level of audit access and provides the highest degree of audit scrutiny. It is reserved for circumstances in which the standard Tier 2 audit has proven insufficient to resolve identified concerns or in which the nature of the issue under investigation requires access to the deepest levels of model internals.

**Access Requirements.** Tier 3 access is cumulative and includes all Tier 1 and Tier 2 access described above, plus the following:

> • Access to model weights and parameter values for all layers of all ClinIQ™ clinical modules, enabling the auditor to directly inspect the learned representations of the models;
>
> • Access to embedding layers and vector representations, including any pre-trained embeddings (e.g., clinical concept embeddings, word embeddings, or image feature embeddings) incorporated into the ClinIQ™ models;
>
> • Access to the training pipeline, including source code for training orchestration, data loading and augmentation routines, loss function definitions, training loop implementation, and any automated hyperparameter tuning or model selection procedures;
>
> • **Critical Constraint:** All Tier 3 access shall be conducted exclusively within Cognitive Foundry\'s secure enclave environment. No model weights, embeddings, training data, training pipeline code, or any other model artifacts may be extracted, copied, downloaded, photographed, or removed from the secure environment under any circumstances. This constraint is fundamental to the protection of Cognitive Foundry\'s intellectual property and is a non-negotiable element of the Tier 3 access protocol.

**Use Cases.** Tier 3 access is typically reserved for cause-based investigations --- that is, audits triggered by specific events or findings that warrant the highest level of scrutiny. Examples of circumstances in which Tier 3 access may be appropriate include:

> • Investigation following a patient-safety incident potentially attributable to ClinIQ™ output;
>
> • Investigation of systematic bias identified during a Tier 2 audit that cannot be fully explained without access to model internals;
>
> • Investigation of unexplained performance degradation that persists despite retraining or recalibration;
>
> • Response to a formal regulatory inquiry or investigation by the New Jersey Department of Health, the FDA, or another regulatory authority; and
>
> • Litigation support in connection with claims involving ClinIQ™ outputs.

Tier 3 is not anticipated as part of the routine annual audit cycle. If triggering conditions are not present, the annual comprehensive audit will be conducted at the Tier 2 level.

**Triggering Conditions.** Langford proposes that Tier 3 audit access may be initiated upon the occurrence of any of the following conditions:

> **(a)** A documented patient-safety event at any Meridian facility that is potentially attributable, in whole or in part, to ClinIQ™ output, as determined by Meridian\'s patient safety committee or risk management function;
>
> **(b)** Consecutive failure of clinical accuracy benchmarks (as defined by the performance thresholds established pursuant to Section 3.3) over two or more quarterly measurement periods;
>
> **(c)** Receipt of a formal regulatory inquiry or investigation from the New Jersey Department of Health, the FDA, or another regulatory authority concerning the ClinIQ™ platform or its outputs; or
>
> **(d)** Mutual written agreement of Meridian and Cognitive Foundry to authorize a Tier 3 audit.

The triggering conditions listed above are proposed for discussion purposes and may be modified during negotiations among the parties. Langford recommends that the final triggering conditions be incorporated into the definitive agreement between Meridian and Cognitive Foundry.
