### TERM 5 — SLA and AI Accuracy Benchmarks

**Agreement sections:** §9.1–9.4 (Service Level Agreement); Exhibit C (SLA/Performance Metrics)
**Meridian priority rank:** 5 | **Vendor priority:** Medium
**Related:** NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act; FDA CDS guidance; Langford audit

#### (i) Proposed Contract Clause Language

> **§9.3 AI Model Performance — Binding Performance Benchmarks.**
>
> (a) **Performance Benchmarks.** Vendor warrants that the Platform's clinical decision-support modules shall meet or exceed the following minimum binding Performance Benchmarks, measured quarterly using a statistically significant prospective sample of Customer's patient encounters and a methodology agreed by the Parties and documented in Exhibit D:
>
> | Clinical Module | Sensitivity (minimum) | Specificity (minimum) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Sepsis Prediction | ≥ 92% | ≥ 88% |
> | Radiology Triage | ≥ 90% | ≥ 85% |
> | Medication-Interaction Alerts | ≥ 95% | ≥ 90% |
>
> (b) **Quarterly Validation.** Vendor shall conduct quarterly validation of each module against the Performance Benchmarks and deliver a written validation report within 30 days of quarter end, including sensitivity/specificity results, methodology, sample size, 95% confidence intervals, and identification of any module below its Benchmark. Validation shall be performed with independent verification by the Independent Auditor (Langford) at Tier 2 access.
>
> (c) **Remediation Plan.** If any module fails its Benchmark in any quarter, Vendor shall deliver a written root-cause analysis and remediation plan within 15 business days of the validation report.
>
> (d) **Escalating Service Credits.** If any module fails its Benchmark in a given quarter, Customer shall be entitled to credits against the next quarterly invoice, in addition to uptime credits under Section 9.2: (i) first quarterly miss (any module): 5% of quarterly fees ($106,875); (ii) second consecutive quarterly miss (same module): 10% ($213,750); (iii) third or subsequent consecutive quarterly miss (same module) or any quarter in which two or more modules fail: 15% ($320,625). Credits reset to 5% after any quarter in which the Benchmark is met. The aggregate of all AI-accuracy credits in any quarter shall not exceed 20% of quarterly fees.
>
> (e) **Termination Right for Persistent Failure.** If any module fails its Benchmark for **three (3) consecutive calendar quarters**, Customer may terminate this Agreement upon 60 days' written notice without payment of any ETF, with full transition assistance under Section 14.5. This right is in addition to, and does not limit, Customer's other termination rights.
>
> **§9.2 Uptime.** Vendor shall maintain Platform Availability of not less than 99.5% per calendar month, with service credits as set forth in Exhibit D (5%/10%/15% of monthly fee tiered by availability). Uptime credits do not limit Customer's other remedies.

#### (ii) Negotiation Rationale and Fallback Ladder

**Positions and red lines.**

| Sub-issue | Vendor opening | Customer opening | Vendor counter (June 30) | Meridian red line |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Benchmark status | Informational only; commercially reasonable efforts | Binding | Informational/aspirational; quarterly reporting only | Must be binding (not informational) |
| Credits | None for accuracy; availability only (≤5%) | 5%/10%/15% escalating | Nominal availability credits only | Credits must cover accuracy |
| Termination trigger | None (executive escalation) | 2 consecutive misses | Resists any SLA termination | No further than 3 consecutive misses |

**Recommended landing zone and why.** The proposal lands at the Playbook fallback (Playbook §8.3): **binding benchmarks** (the red line — benchmarks must be binding, not informational), **escalating credits 5%/10%/15%**, and **termination after 3 consecutive quarterly misses** (a concession from Customer's opening of 2 misses, per the Playbook fallback and Mr. Delgado's guidance to "be prepared to accept the 3-miss termination trigger per playbook"). The aggregate quarterly cap of 20% of quarterly fees bridges the Vendor position (15%, $320,625) and Customer position (25%, $534,375). Uptime is set at 99.5% (Vendor's counter; the credit structure is the principal concern, not the uptime threshold). The benchmarks themselves (sepsis 92%/88%, radiology 90%/85%, med-interaction 95%/90%) are clinically calibrated by Meridian's CMO and are not subject to negotiation under this fallback — only the remedies are.

**Fallback ladder:**

1. **Primary (proposed):** Binding benchmarks; 5%/10%/15% escalating credits; 20% aggregate quarterly cap; termination after 3 consecutive misses; 15-business-day remediation plan; Langford Tier 2 validation.
2. **If Vendor resists binding status:** Hold binding status as a red line (Playbook §8.4(a) — "the distinction between a binding benchmark and an aspirational target is the difference between a contractual commitment and a marketing claim"). Offer to narrow the termination trigger to 3 consecutive misses on the same module (already in the proposal) and accept the 15% aggregate cap as a fallback on credits only.
3. **If Vendor resists the termination trigger:** Hold at 3 consecutive misses (Playbook §8.4(b) — no further concession authorized). Offer executive escalation as an interim step before termination (SLA Governance Committee review after 2 consecutive misses, termination after 3).
4. **Red line:** Benchmarks must be binding; termination trigger no further than 3 consecutive quarterly misses. Below this, escalate to GC.

#### (iii) Acceptability Analysis for Vendor Team

**To Sandra Hu:** The 3-miss termination trigger (vs. Customer's 2-miss opening) gives Vendor a full additional quarter to remediate before termination exposure — a meaningful concession. The 20% aggregate credit cap bounds Vendor's quarterly financial exposure. The benchmarks themselves are the same targets Vendor already publishes as "non-binding" in its standards summary and quarterly reports — making them binding changes the remedy, not the target. Vendor's own standards summary commits to quarterly performance reporting; the proposal adds consequences to reporting Vendor already does.

**To Rafael Bowen:** Bowen's likely objection is that AI accuracy is "inherently variable" and cannot be guaranteed. Response: the benchmarks are measured quarterly on a statistically significant prospective sample with 95% confidence intervals and an agreed methodology — they account for population variability rather than ignoring it. The remediation-plan mechanism (15 business days) gives Vendor a cure path before credits escalate. The termination trigger requires three consecutive misses (a year of persistent underperformance), not a single bad quarter — a high bar that protects Vendor from transient variability while protecting Meridian from a persistently unsafe system. Patient-safety context (sepsis false negatives, missed radiology findings) makes binding benchmarks a clinical-safety imperative, not a commercial preference.

**Likely objection and response.** Hu/Bowen will push to keep metrics informational. Response: an AI system generating clinical decisions for ~2.1M annual encounters across 14 hospitals cannot have purely aspirational accuracy commitments; the Playbook red line is binding status, and the 3-miss trigger is the concession that should close the gap.

#### (iv) Regulatory and Compliance Constraints

- **HIPAA.** Quarterly validation uses a statistically significant prospective sample of Customer's patient encounters; any patient data used in validation must be de-identified or handled in compliance with the BAA (Exhibit F) and HIPAA Privacy/Security Rules. Bias and fairness testing across demographic subgroups (race, ethnicity, sex, age, etc.) must comply with HIPAA de-identification standards (Safe Harbor or Expert Determination) so that subgroup performance analysis does not impermissibly disclose PHI. The agreed Exhibit E security framework (encryption, access controls, audit logging) governs validation data handling.
- **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act.** The Act's annual audit (§5) requires assessment of validation results and ongoing performance metrics; the quarterly validation reports and Langford Tier 2 verification feed directly into NJ Act compliance. Bias and fairness testing across demographic subgroups (required by the Act §4(a)(4)) should be reported quarterly as informational SLA components in the Transparency Report (Section 10.1). The Act does not create a private right of action but does not preempt malpractice claims — binding benchmarks that detect performance degradation before patient harm reduce Meridian's litigation exposure.
- **FDA clinical decision-support guidance.** Binding accuracy benchmarks support Meridian's clinical governance and any FDA CDS exemption analysis (the four-criteria test under 21st Century Cures Act §3060, particularly the "independent review of the basis for recommendations" criterion). The radiology module's interaction with medical imaging raises the first-criterion question that Langford's regulatory compliance verification will assess.
- **Patient-safety linkage.** SLA accuracy is the operational complement to the Term 1 patient-safety super-cap: binding benchmarks detect performance degradation before it produces patient harm, and the termination trigger gives Meridian an exit from a persistently underperforming system. The two terms should be presented as a coherent clinical-safety package.
- **Measurement methodology.** Validation per NIST AI 100-1 guidelines, 95% confidence intervals, prospective evaluation on actual clinical encounters (Meridian's preference per Playbook §8.2), with Langford independent verification.

### TERM 6 — IP Ownership (Model Weights, Fine-Tuned Adaptations, Output Data)

**Agreement sections:** §6.1–6.5 (Intellectual Property Ownership); §2.4–2.5 (Output License; Portability)
**Meridian priority rank:** 6 | **Vendor priority:** Medium
**Related:** Langford audit framework (Term 2)

#### (i) Proposed Contract Clause Language

> **§6.1 Vendor IP.** Vendor retains all right, title, and interest in and to the Platform, all Model IP (including base model weights, architecture, fine-tuned adaptations, parameters, embeddings, training methodologies, and Derivative Data), all Updates, and all improvements and derivative works thereof, whether developed before, during, or after the Term. No transfer of ownership of any Vendor IP is intended or effected under this Agreement.
>
> **§6.2 Fine-Tuned Adaptations; Customer License.**
>
> (a) **Vendor Ownership.** All fine-tuned model weights, parameters, configurations, embeddings, and Derivative Data generated through the operation of the Platform are and shall remain the sole and exclusive property of Vendor.
>
> (b) **Irrevocable Perpetual License to Customer.** Vendor hereby grants Customer a non-exclusive, **perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, fully paid-up** license to use, copy, modify, and create derivative works from (i) all Outputs generated by the Platform from Customer Data, and (ii) all derivative data sets generated by the Platform from Customer Data (excluding base model weights, model architecture, and proprietary algorithms), for any internal clinical, operational, regulatory, legal, compliance, and quality-improvement purpose, including use in connection with any successor platform or clinical decision-support system. This license shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement for any reason, including termination for cause, and may not be revoked by Vendor for any reason.
>
> (c) **Portability.** Upon termination or expiration for any reason, Vendor shall export and deliver to Customer all Customer Data, Outputs, and derivative data sets (excluding base model weights, model architecture, and proprietary algorithms) in FHIR R4, HL7 v2, and such other machine-readable, interoperable formats as Customer may reasonably specify, sufficient to enable Customer to use such data with a successor platform. Vendor shall provide reasonable technical documentation and assistance to enable Customer to exercise its portability rights.
>
> (d) **No Assignment.** Customer has no ownership interest in any model weights, parameters, embeddings, or model architectures, whether base or fine-tuned. Customer does not assign, and is not obligated to assign, any intellectual property rights to Vendor with respect to Customer Pre-Existing IP.
>
> **§6.3 Output License.** Customer's rights in Outputs are as set forth in Section 2.4 and Section 6.2(b). The Output License is perpetual, irrevocable, and royalty-free, and extends to derivative data sets generated from Customer Data.
>
> **§6.5 Customer Pre-Existing IP.** Vendor acknowledges Customer's ownership of its pre-existing intellectual property (clinical protocols, pathways, order sets, formularies, operational data, MeridianConnect configurations). Nothing in this Agreement grants Vendor any rights in Customer Pre-Existing IP except the limited right to access and use it as necessary to perform its obligations.

#### (ii) Negotiation Rationale and Fallback Ladder

**Positions and red lines.**

| Sub-issue | Vendor opening | Customer opening | Vendor counter (June 30) | Meridian red line |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Model weights/architecture | Vendor owns all; output license only | Customer owns fine-tuned weights + derivative data | Vendor owns all; CTO "hard no" on weights access; enhanced output license | Must retain meaningful rights to fine-tuned adaptations; portability essential |
| Output data | Non-exclusive, non-transferable license | Full ownership of outputs | Perpetual, irrevocable output license; retains aggregated output use | Outputs from Customer data = Customer property or equivalent |
| Portability | None; data returned/destroyed | Full portability of fine-tuned weights + data | Data portability (Customer Data only, not weights); open to perpetual license | Portability essential for termination/transition |

**Recommended landing zone and why.** This proposal lands at the Playbook second/final fallback (Playbook §4.4): **Vendor ownership of all Model IP** (including fine-tuned weights), with Customer receiving an **irrevocable, perpetual, fully paid-up license** to Outputs and derivative data sets, plus **portability** of Customer Data, Outputs, and derivative data sets. Mr. Delgado's guidance is explicit: "accept the playbook fallback position if Vendor agrees to portability and the Langford audit framework is in place. Raw weight access/ownership is the CTO's 'hard no' — this is not where we should spend negotiation capital, and the perpetual license with portability gives us what we functionally need." The CTO's "hard no" on raw model weights (base and, per Vendor's counter, fine-tuned) makes ownership or extractable portability of weights unattainable; the perpetual license + data portability + Langford Tier 3 enclave (Term 2) together provide Meridian's functional needs: continuity of access to the value created from its data, an exit path, and a technical-review mechanism for the weights.

**Fallback ladder:**

1. **Primary (proposed):** Vendor owns all Model IP; Customer receives irrevocable perpetual license to Outputs + derivative data sets; portability of Customer Data/Outputs/derivative data sets in interoperable formats; Langford Tier 3 enclave as the weights-review mechanism.
2. **If Vendor moves on fine-tuned weights:** Seek portability of fine-tuned model weights (the delta between base and fine-tuned) for use on alternative infrastructure — the Playbook's preferred outcome. This is a stretch goal given the CTO hard no; pursue only if Vendor offers.
3. **If Vendor resists perpetual/irrevocable license:** Hold irrevocable + perpetual + survives termination as a red line (Playbook §4.4 — "irrevocable, meaning it cannot be terminated by the vendor for any reason, including breach"). A revocable or term-limited license is unacceptable.
4. **Red line:** Must include perpetual license + portability. If Vendor refuses, escalate to GC (Playbook §4.4 — "potential deal termination").

#### (iii) Acceptability Analysis for Vendor Team

**To Sandra Hu:** The proposal concedes the central IP-ownership question entirely in Vendor's favor — Vendor owns all Model IP, including fine-tuned weights, which is Vendor's opening position and its CTO's hard line. This is a significant Meridian concession on a term where Meridian's opening sought Customer ownership of fine-tuned weights. In exchange, Meridian asks only for what Vendor's own counter already offered (perpetual, irrevocable output license) plus data portability of Customer Data and derivative data sets (excluding model weights/architecture). Hu can accept this as consistent with Vendor's stated positions.

**To Rafael Bowen:** Bowen's IP-protection concern is fully met: no transfer of model weights, architecture, or proprietary algorithms; no extractable access to weights (the Langford Tier 3 enclave in Term 2 is the only weights-review mechanism, and it is no-extraction). The portability obligation is limited to Customer Data, Outputs, and derivative data sets — not Model IP — and is the same data Meridian would need for any vendor transition. The irrevocable/perpetual license is to Outputs and derivative data sets (the results of processing Meridian's own data), not to Vendor's models.

**Likely objection and response.** Bowen may resist the "irrevocable" qualifier (preferring a license that terminates on breach). Response: irrevocability is the Playbook red line and is justified because Meridian's clinical records and regulatory filings will embed Outputs indefinitely; a revocable license would create retrospective compliance risk. The license is to Outputs/derivative data, not to Vendor's models, so irrevocability does not compromise Vendor's IP.

#### (iv) Regulatory and Compliance Constraints

- **HIPAA.** Outputs and derivative data sets that contain PHI remain subject to the BAA; the perpetual license and portability obligations operate within the BAA's PHI framework. De-identification standards apply to any aggregated/derivative data Vendor retains.
- **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act.** The Act does not require Meridian to own model weights, but it does require access to model documentation (§7(a)(1)) — satisfied by the Term 2 audit/transparency framework, not by IP ownership. The perpetual license to Outputs supports Meridian's ongoing transparency and patient-notification obligations.
- **FDA.** If the Platform is FDA-regulated, Vendor's ownership of Model IP includes the obligation to maintain clearances (Playbook §10.3); the IP structure does not shift FDA obligations to Meridian.
- **Portability/interoperability.** FHIR R4/HL7 v2 export of Customer Data and Outputs supports Meridian's interoperability obligations and avoids vendor lock-in — the strategic rationale for the Playbook's portability requirement (§4.1).

### TERM 7 — Indemnification Scope and Defense Procedure

**Agreement sections:** §13.1–13.7 (Indemnification); §15.1 (Insurance)
**Meridian priority rank:** 7 (lowest) | **Vendor priority:** High
**Related:** Exhibit F (BAA); Article 15 / Exhibit G (Insurance); Term 1 (super-cap)

#### (i) Proposed Contract Clause Language

> **§13.1 Vendor Indemnification.** Vendor shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless Customer and its Affiliates and their respective officers, directors, employees, agents, and medical staff members (collectively, "Customer Indemnitees") from and against any and all third-party claims, governmental investigations, regulatory enforcement actions, suits, proceedings, demands, judgments, damages, liabilities, penalties, fines, losses, costs, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' and expert-witness fees) (collectively, "Losses") arising out of or related to:
>
> (a) **IP Infringement** — any allegation that Customer's authorized use of the Platform infringes or misappropriates any third-party patent, copyright, trademark, or trade secret;
>
> (b) **Data Breach** — any claim, regulatory action, or proceeding arising from a Security Incident or Breach of Unsecured PHI (as defined in the BAA) attributable to Vendor, its subprocessors, or its systems, including notification, credit-monitoring, forensic-investigation, and regulatory-fine costs;
>
> (c) **Regulatory Penalties** — any civil penalty, fine, or enforcement action by a governmental authority (including HHS OCR, the NJ Department of Health, the FDA, or any state attorney general) arising from Vendor's acts or omissions in connection with the Platform, including non-compliance with HIPAA, HITECH, the NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act, or applicable FDA regulations, to the extent such penalty is attributable to Vendor's breach of its documentation, audit-cooperation, or regulatory-cooperation obligations under this Agreement; and
>
> (d) **Patient-Harm from AI-Output Errors** — any claim alleging bodily injury, clinical misdiagnosis, treatment error, or other patient harm caused by or materially contributed to by an Output, recommendation, alert, or decision-support result generated by the Platform that is alleged to be inaccurate, misleading, biased, delayed, or otherwise deficient, where the proximate cause of the harm is an erroneous, misleading, or defective AI-generated output.
>
> **§13.5 Defense Control — Shared Defense for Patient-Safety Claims.**
>
> (a) **General.** For claims under Section 13.1(a), (b), and (c), the Indemnifying Party (Vendor) shall have primary control of the defense and settlement, provided that no settlement shall impose liability or admit fault on behalf of the Indemnified Party without its prior written consent (not to be unreasonably withheld).
>
> (b) **Patient-Safety Claims — Shared Defense.** For claims under Section 13.1(d) (Patient-Harm from AI-Output Errors): (i) Vendor shall be primarily responsible for the defense and indemnification of claims directly attributable to AI-output errors; (ii) Customer shall be primarily responsible for claims arising from clinician override or independent clinical judgment; (iii) for mixed-causation claims, the Parties shall cooperate in a shared defense, each bearing its proportionate share of defense costs and indemnification obligations in good faith, with allocation determined through the dispute-resolution process in Section 16.2 if the Parties cannot agree; (iv) neither Party shall settle a patient-safety claim without the other Party's prior written consent; and (v) Customer shall have the right to participate with counsel of its choosing at its own expense, and Vendor shall not settle any patient-safety claim in a manner that adversely affects Customer's clinical reputation without Customer's consent.
>
> **§13.7 Indemnification Cap.** Vendor's aggregate indemnification liability under this Article 13 shall be subject to the Super-Cap set forth in Section 12.3 ($28,800,000 as of the first Contract Year), except that indemnification for Regulatory Penalties under Section 13.1(c) arising from Vendor's breach of its NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act obligations shall not be subject to the General Cap and shall be subject to the Super-Cap. Vendor's indemnification obligations under Section 13.1(a) (IP) are eligible for the Super-Cap per Section 12.3(b).

#### (ii) Negotiation Rationale and Fallback Ladder

**Positions and red lines.**

| Sub-issue | Vendor opening | Customer opening | Vendor counter (June 30) | Meridian red line |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indemnity scope | IP infringement only | IP + regulatory + patient harm + data breach | IP + data breach only (declines patient-harm, regulatory) | Must include patient harm + IP at minimum |
| Defense control | Vendor controls all | Customer controls patient-safety | Vendor controls; offers joint defense | Customer meaningful control over patient-safety defense |
| Cap | = general liability cap | Excluded from general cap | (linked to cap dispute) | Never less than super-cap floor |

**Recommended landing zone and why.** This is Meridian's lowest-priority term (Playbook §2.5: "the most readily tradeable"), and the Playbook trade-off guidance (§7.3) authorizes moderate flexibility on indemnification scope where Vendor offers meaningful concessions on patient-safety liability (Term 1) or audit rights (Term 2). The proposal lands at the Playbook fallback (§7.2): **shared defense with Vendor primary for AI-output claims**. On scope, the proposal secures the Meridian red line (patient-harm + IP at minimum) plus data breach (Vendor concedes) plus regulatory penalties (supported by the NJ Act, which assesses penalties against Meridian as covered entity — making Vendor indemnification for Vendor-attributable penalties essential). The defense-control concession (from Customer-controls to shared/Vendor-primary) is the principal Meridian concession, justified by the priority ranking and the Term 1 super-cap that backstops patient-safety exposure. The proposal is designed to work as a coherent liability package with Term 1: the super-cap (Term 1) is the indemnification cap (Term 7 §13.7), and the patient-safety carve-out (Term 1) and patient-harm indemnity (Term 7) cover the same risk from cap and indemnity angles.

**Fallback ladder:**

1. **Primary (proposed):** IP + data breach + regulatory penalties + patient-harm (AI-output) indemnity; shared defense, Vendor primary for AI-output claims; subject to super-cap.
2. **If Vendor resists patient-harm indemnity:** Hold patient-harm + IP as the red-line minimum (Playbook §7.4). Offer to narrow patient-harm indemnity to claims where the AI output was the proximate cause (already in the proposal) and to accept shared defense (already in the proposal). Eliminating patient-harm indemnity entirely requires GC escalation (Playbook §7.3).
3. **If Vendor resists regulatory-penalty indemnity:** Note that the NJ Act assesses penalties against Meridian (covered entity) for Vendor-attributable non-compliance; without indemnity, Meridian bears regulatory exposure for Vendor's breach. Hold regulatory-penalty indemnity for Vendor-attributable NJ Act/HIPAA/FDA penalties, carved out of the general cap.
4. **If Vendor insists on Customer defense control for all patient-safety claims:** Accept shared defense (Vendor primary for AI-output, Customer primary for clinician-override) as the fallback; do not concede Customer-controlled defense of all patient-safety claims unless paired with a major Term 1 concession.
5. **Red line:** Must include patient harm + IP at minimum; never less than super-cap floor. Eliminating patient-harm indemnity requires GC escalation.

#### (iii) Acceptability Analysis for Vendor Team

**To Sandra Hu:** The scope expansion (adding data breach, regulatory penalties, and patient-harm) is calibrated: data breach is already conceded by Vendor; regulatory-penalty indemnity is limited to penalties attributable to Vendor's own breach of its documentation/audit/cooperation obligations (not Meridian's operational failures); and patient-harm indemnity is limited to claims where the AI output was the proximate cause (excluding clinician-override cases, which remain Customer's responsibility). The shared-defense structure gives Vendor primary control of AI-output claims (Vendor's preferred position) while preserving Customer participation and settlement-consent rights for reputation-sensitive patient-safety claims.

**To Rafael Bowen:** Bowen's concern is open-ended liability exposure. The proposal bounds it: indemnification is subject to the Term 1 super-cap ($28.8M), not uncapped; patient-harm indemnity is proximate-cause-limited (excluding clinician-override/independent-judgment cases, which Vendor's standards summary correctly identifies as clinical-malpractice matters outside a software vendor's scope); and the shared-defense structure allocates clinician-override claims to Customer. The regulatory-penalty indemnity is tied to Vendor's own contractual obligations (documentation, audit cooperation) — Vendor controls whether it breaches these. The package is coherent with Term 1's cap structure, avoiding double-exposure.

**Likely objection and response.** Bowen will resist patient-harm and regulatory indemnity as outside a software vendor's scope. Response: (a) patient-harm indemnity is proximate-cause-limited to AI-output errors — the category where Vendor's product, not Meridian's clinicians, caused the harm; (b) regulatory-penalty indemnity is limited to Vendor-attributable non-compliance, and the NJ Act's penalty regime (assessed against Meridian) makes this indemnity essential for Meridian to accept the regulatory risk of deploying Vendor's Platform; (c) both are capped at the super-cap, bounding exposure. The board risk committee's Tier 1 designation means Meridian cannot accept a deal where Vendor does not stand behind its product's clinical outputs.

#### (iv) Regulatory and Compliance Constraints

- **NJ AI in Healthcare Transparency Act.** Penalties (up to $50,000/violation, per-day accrual) are assessed against Meridian as the covered entity, not Vendor. Where Meridian incurs penalties because Vendor failed to provide required documentation, audit access, or regulatory cooperation, Meridian's only recourse is contractual — making the §13.1(c) regulatory-penalty indemnity essential. Whitfield Ames recommends this indemnity be carved out of the general cap (or subject to the super-cap), given the per-day accrual mechanism could produce substantial aggregate exposure. The proposal places it at the super-cap. The Act does not create a private right of action but expressly does not preempt existing state or federal causes of action (medical malpractice, negligence, products liability, NJ Consumer Fraud Act); evidence of Act non-compliance could be admissible in such litigation, reinforcing the need for the §13.1(d) patient-harm indemnity to cover AI-output-deficiency claims that may surface in malpractice actions.
- **HIPAA.** Data-breach indemnity (§13.1(b)) complements the BAA; the BAA governs PHI obligations while the indemnity provides third-party-claim defense. Meridian's 24-hour breach-notification standard and the BAA's breach framework apply. The agreed Exhibit E security framework (AES-256 encryption, customer-managed CMKs, RBAC + MFA, 7-year audit logging) backstops the HIPAA Security Rule obligations underlying the data-breach indemnity.
- **Pennsylvania Breach of Personal Information Notification Act (73 Pa. Stat. § 2303).** Applies to personal information of individuals associated with Meridian's six (6) Pennsylvania facilities. A data breach affecting PA-facility personal information triggers Vendor's breach-notification and cooperation obligations under Exhibit D §8.3 and the §13.1(b) data-breach indemnity; Vendor must cooperate with notification to affected individuals, the Pennsylvania Attorney General, and other authorities.
- **FDA.** Where the Platform is FDA-regulated, Vendor's failure to maintain clearances or to notify Meridian of adverse FDA action creates regulatory exposure; the §13.1(c) regulatory-penalty indemnity and the Playbook §10.3 FDA cooperation/indemnification provisions align.
- **Board Risk Committee.** Although Term 7 is Meridian's lowest priority, the Tier 1 enterprise-risk designation means indemnification terms will receive board scrutiny. The proposal's preservation of patient-harm indemnity and regulatory-penalty indemnity is defensible in that context; eliminating patient-harm indemnity would not be.
- **Insurance backstop.** The Term 1 insurance commitment ($30M/$60M by March 31, 2026 with step-in rights) is the collectability backstop for the indemnification obligations; the two terms must be presented together.

## IV. Concession Sequencing and Package Strategy for July 18

### A. Sequencing

The July 18 session should be sequenced to deploy concessions strategically:

1. **Open with Terms 1 and 2 (patient-safety liability, audit rights).** Frame audit rights as a regulatory compliance baseline (NJ Act), not a commercial preference — per Whitfield Ames's recommendation to reframe the discussion. Use comparable-deal intelligence on the liability cap carefully (anchor on "market practice," do not disclose source). Hold the patient-safety 3× ACV floor as non-negotiable.

2. **Move to Term 3 (termination).** Present the graduated ETF as the workable framework both sides' principals have signaled. Pair it with robust transition and data-export obligations — these terms travel together.

3. **Deploy Term 4 (data usage) as concession currency.** Hold the opening per-use-case-consent position through the early rounds. Once Vendor has moved on Terms 1–3, offer the calibrated 45-day opt-out + retraining-alternative package in exchange for closing the remaining gaps on Terms 1–3. This is the term where Meridian has leverage (Vendor's #1 priority) and should spend it last.

4. **Close Terms 5, 6, 7.** Term 5 (SLA) lands at the Playbook fallback (binding benchmarks, 3-miss trigger). Term 6 (IP) lands at the Playbook final fallback (Vendor ownership + perpetual license + portability) — present as a Meridian concession. Term 7 (indemnification) lands at shared defense with the scope red lines preserved.

### B. Package Approach

The Playbook's critical rule (§2.5) prohibits conceding a higher-priority item for a lower-priority one without GC approval. The package approach at the table should be: **concessions flow downhill** — Meridian concedes on Term 4 (data usage, Vendor's #1) and Term 6 (IP ownership) to secure Terms 1–3 (patient-safety, audit, termination). Concessions on Term 7 (indemnification defense control) are available only if Term 1 (patient-safety super-cap) is secured at or above the 3× ACV floor.

### C. Items Requiring GC Escalation Before Final Acceptance

The following proposed landing zones deviate from Playbook absolute floors/red lines and require Dr. Narayanan's GC-level approval (and, for the patient-safety floor, Board Risk Committee concurrence) before Meridian communicates acceptance:

| Term | Deviation | Playbook reference | Escalation to |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General cap at 1.5× ACV paired with 3× patient-safety super-cap (cross-row pairing) | §3.3 (coordinated package) | GC |
| 3 | Graduated ETF (any ETF structure) | §9.4 / §12.2 (no ETF under any structure) | GC (presumptive rejection absent GC override) |
| 4 | 45-day opt-out (exceeds 30-day red line) | §6.4(c) | GC |
| 7 | Shared defense (concession from Customer-controlled) | §7.2 (fallback) | Within Playbook fallback — GC concurrence recommended |

The patient-safety 3× ACV floor (Term 1) is held and does not require escalation; if Vendor refuses it, escalation to the Board Risk Committee is mandatory (Playbook §3.2.4, §12.2).

---

## V. Open Items and Next Steps

1. **Comparable-deal intelligence surfacing.** Mr. Delgado has flagged the open question of whether to reference the comparable-deal intelligence directly ("we understand you've agreed to 1.5× ACV in comparable deals") or indirectly ("market practice for deals of this scope supports caps at 1.5–2× ACV"). Ms. Sutter's tactical read is requested before the July 18 session. Recommendation: indirect anchoring in the first round; direct reference (without source disclosure) only if Vendor holds at 1× and the session stalls.

2. **Board Risk Committee briefing.** Reserve a briefing slot the week of August 4, 2025 to present the final liability/indemnification package (Terms 1, 5, 7) and the audit/transparency framework (Term 2) before the August 15 target execution. Dr. Narayanan to manage the calendar.

3. **Langford engagement.** Initiate Langford engagement-letter discussions in parallel with the Agreement negotiation so the audit engagement is consistent with the Term 2 contractual audit rights and the first annual audit can commence within 90 days of the January 6, 2026 go-live (Langford recommendation), with fieldwork by Q4 2026 to meet the January 6, 2027 statutory deadline.

4. **Exhibit D reconciliation.** The Term 4 compromise language resolves the bracketed Alternative A/B in Exhibit D §5.2 and the bracketed alternatives in §6.2 (data return timeline). Exhibit D should be conformed to the agreed provisions; the order of precedence (BAA > Exhibit D > Agreement for PHI) is preserved.

5. **Insurance verification.** Whitfield Ames to verify the Northfield Mutual carrier rating (A.M. Best A- or better) and policy details before execution, and to confirm the broker's written commitment to seek the $30M/$60M increase at the 2026 renewal.

6. **Patient-notification operationalization.** Convene the cross-functional working group (GC, Compliance, Patient Experience, Clinical Operations, Health IT) no later than August 2025 to design and test the NJ Act patient-notification workflow before the January 6, 2026 go-live.

---

It does not constitute legal advice to any other person or entity. The analysis is based on the source documents referenced herein and the facts known as of July 14, 2025. All proposed clause language is draft compromise language for the July 18, 2025 joint negotiation session and is subject to revision based on negotiation outcomes and General Counsel review.*", "file_path": "compromise-proposals.md"}

        print("TERM 5 (iv) HIPAA bullet FOUND:")
        print("  ", line.strip()[:300])
        break
else:
    print("NOT FOUND in Term 5 (iv)")
print()
# Also confirm the no-private-right text is in Term 1 and Term 7 (iv)
for idx,label in [(1,"Term 1"),(7,"Term 7")]:
    sec = iv[idx]
    has = "private right of action" in sec.lower() and "does not preempt" in sec.lower()
    print(f"{label} (iv) has no-private-right/preempt text: {'YES' if has else 'NO'}")
# Confirm PA breach in Term 4 and Term 7 (iv)
for idx,label in [(4,"Term 4"),(7,"Term 7")]:
    sec = iv[idx]
    has = "73 pa. stat" in sec.lower() or "pennsylvania breach" in sec.lower()
    print(f"{label} (iv) has PA breach notification: {'YES' if has else 'NO'}")
# Confirm §10.6 cost allocation in Term 2
term2 = re.split(r'TERM 2 —', D)[1].split('TERM 3 —')[0]
print(f"Term 2 has §10.6 cost allocation: {'YES' if '§10.6' in term2 and 'cost allocation' in term2.lower() else 'NO'}")
print(f"Term 2 has Langford costs $180-280K: {'YES' if '$180,000' in term2 and '$280,000' in term2 else 'NO'}")
PYEOF"}

text:
The completeness audit is complete and all gaps are filled. Here is the summary of the audit.

## Completeness Audit — Results

I re-read the key source files (Contested Terms Matrix with all 21 sub-issues, the Sutter/Delgado emails, the Playbook, the NJ Act brief, the Langford proposal, and Exhibits C/D) and cross-checked every distinct item against the actual deliverable content by extracting and searching the rendered `.docx` text.

### What was already covered (verified present)
- All **21 sub-issues** across the 7 terms (general cap, super-cap carve-outs, insurance floor, model weights, output license, portability, audit tiered access, NJ Act compliance, auditor confidentiality, aggregated-data use, opt-out/deletion, Exhibit D cross-ref, indemnity scope, defense control, insurance backstop, accuracy benchmarks, service credits, termination trigger, ETF, transition, data return)
- All **4 required components** per term (clause language / rationale+fallback ladder / acceptability for Hu & Bowen / regulatory constraints) — both vendor counsel addressed in all 7 terms
- Dr. Narayanan's **4 absolute red lines**, comparable-deal intelligence, Langford tiered framework, insurance gap with March 31 2026 date, Board Tier 1 flag, concession sequencing, GC escalation flags, all 6 open items
- NJ Act specifics (90-day report, 15-business-day DOH production, Jan 6 2027 first audit, anti-waiver, per-day penalties, covered-entity penalty allocation, mandatory suspension, multi-state monitoring)

### 5 genuine gaps found and filled
1. **Term 5 (SLA) (iv) was missing HIPAA** — Sutter requires all 3 regimes (HIPAA/NJ Act/FDA) across terms. Added a HIPAA bullet covering validation-data PHI handling, de-identification for bias testing, and the Exhibit E security backstop. *(Now all 7 terms have all 3 regimes.)*
2. **Audit cost-sharing dispute not addressed** — the Matrix flags Vendor "seeks cost-sharing on NJ-specific audit activities," but I'd only said "at Customer's expense." Added a new **§10.6 Audit Cost Allocation** clause (Customer bears routine Tier 2; Vendor bears cause-based Tier 3 + facilitation; NJ-Act-specific costs shared equally) plus the **Langford cost figures** ($180–220K Tier 2, $240–280K Tier 3, $35–50K Tier 1) and a cost-allocation fallback ladder rung.
3. **PA Breach Notification Act (73 Pa. Stat. § 2303)** — Exhibit D §8.3; applies to Meridian's 6 PA facilities. Added to the cross-cutting regulatory backdrop and to Term 4 (iv) and Term 7 (iv).
4. **NJ Act "no private right of action but doesn't preempt malpractice/negligence/products liability/NJ Consumer Fraud Act"** — Whitfield Ames brief; non-compliance evidence admissible in litigation. Added to the cross-cutting backdrop and to Term 1 (iv) and Term 7 (iv), reinforcing the patient-safety super-cap and patient-harm indemnity.
5. **Agreed security framework (Exhibit E)** — AES-256/TLS 1.2+/customer-managed CMKs/RBAC+MFA/7-year audit logs backstop the HIPAA Security Rule. Added as context in the cross-cutting backdrop and the relevant (iv) sections (not treated as a contested issue, since it is agreed in both drafts).

### Final validation
- `output/compromise-proposals.docx` — **valid** (ECMA-376 schema, ZIP integrity, rId consistency)
- 279 paragraphs, 12 tables, 56 block-quoted clause-language paragraphs, 53 headings
- All 21 fact-checks pass; all 7 terms have all 3 regulatory regimes; both vendor counsel addressed in every term

The deliverable is complete.
